proposals.title: Structural Reinforcement of Hydrolytically Sensitive Functional Materials via a MOF Scaffold: A Study on Water-Stable Organic Proton Conductors
proposals.motivation: Many high-performance functional materials, such as organic crystalline proton conductors, suffer from structural degradation or loss of porosity under humid conditions, limiting their practical application. While MOFs are typically explored for their intrinsic functionality (e.g., sorption, catalysis), their potential as robust, porous scaffolds to physically stabilize and protect sensitive guest materials remains underexplored. This approach could unlock the use of fragile but highly active molecular systems in demanding environments.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that embedding hydrolytically sensitive, high-conductivity organic crystals within the rigid, hydrophobic pores of a water-stable MOF will create a composite where the MOF acts primarily as a protective structural scaffold, thereby preserving the guest's superior proton conduction properties under dynamic humidity and temperature cycling.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a composite by in-situ crystallization of a model organic proton conductor (e.g., imidazole or triazole derivatives) within the one-dimensional channels of a hydrophobic, ultramicroporous MOF scaffold like ZIF-8 or a robust, diamondoid-topology hybrid ultramicroporous material. The composite's structural integrity will be monitored using in-situ variable-humidity PXRD and dynamic water vapor sorption. Proton conductivity will be measured via AC impedance spectroscopy under controlled humidity (20-90% RH) and elevated temperatures (up to 120°C), comparing the composite to the pure organic crystal.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new design paradigm for composite materials where MOFs provide passive, structural reinforcement rather than active functionality. It will demonstrate a pathway to engineer water stability into otherwise fragile organic functional materials. The findings will contribute directly to the development of durable solid electrolytes for proton-exchange membrane fuel cells operating under low-humidity conditions.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in deliberately inverting the conventional role of MOFs from active functional component to passive, protective host, a concept distinct from typical MOF-composite strategies focused on synergistic catalysis or drug delivery. This leverages the intrinsic mechanical and hydrothermal stability of certain MOF topologies for a fundamentally different purpose.

proposals.title: Bio-inspired Active Site Engineering: Designing a Non-Platinum ORR Catalyst via a Computationally-Guided Metal-Organic Framework Scaffold
proposals.motivation: The high cost and scarcity of platinum are major barriers to the widespread adoption of fuel cells. Inspired by enzymes that use protein scaffolds to confer high activity on abundant metals, this project seeks to break the reliance on platinum for the oxygen reduction reaction (ORR). We propose to design a synthetic, rigid, and tunable porous scaffold—a metal-organic framework (MOF)—to precisely control the geometry and electronic environment of a cheap, first-row transition metal center, mimicking the function of Pt.
proposals.hypothesis: A rationally designed, ultramicroporous MOF scaffold can impose a specific coordination geometry and electronic structure on an abundant metal ion (e.g., Fe, Co, or Ni), creating an active site with catalytic activity for ORR rivaling that of platinum-based materials.
proposals.method: We will employ density functional theory (DFT) to screen potential MOF nodes and linker combinations, identifying those that can impose a distorted, low-coordination geometry on the metal center, optimizing the d-band center for O2 adsorption and activation. Based on this, we will synthesize targeted, rigid diamondoid-topology hybrid ultramicroporous materials, incorporating the selected metal ions via post-synthetic modification. The materials will be characterized in-depth (PXRD, XAS, gas sorption) and their ORR performance evaluated electrochemically under high-temperature, dynamic conditions relevant to operational fuel cells.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a concrete design principle for replacing precious metals in electrocatalysis through scaffold-based active site control, directly contributing to sustainable energy technology. It will also expand the functional application of Kitagawa-type soft and rigid porous crystals beyond sorption and separation into the realm of precise electrocatalytic site engineering.
proposals.novelty: While MOFs are known as supports for nanoparticles, the novelty lies in using the MOF's intrinsic, rigid coordination chemistry as the *primary* design element to create a *single-atom* non-precious metal catalyst with a pre-defined electronic structure, a direct materials analogy to enzymatic active site engineering.

proposals.title: Engineering Local Disorder in Amorphous Conjugated Microporous Polymers for Platinum-Free Photocatalytic Hydrogen Evolution
proposals.motivation: Platinum remains the benchmark co-catalyst for photocatalytic hydrogen evolution, but its scarcity and cost hinder scalability. Concurrently, the field of porous materials, including metal-organic frameworks (MOFs), has shown that amorphous or flexible phases can exhibit unique sorption and recognition properties distinct from their crystalline forms. This project investigates whether similar principles of engineered local disorder can be applied to metal-free polymer photocatalysts to eliminate platinum dependence.
proposals.hypothesis: The flexible, defect-rich local environments in amorphous conjugated microporous polymers (a-CMPs) can be systematically tailored to create highly active, metal-free catalytic sites for proton reduction, with performance rivaling platinum-loaded crystalline analogues by optimizing charge separation and reactant binding dynamics.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a series of a-CMPs using high-temperature dynamic covalent chemistry (e.g., Sonogashira-Hagihara coupling) to create polymers with identical linkages but varying local structure, controlled by polymerization kinetics and monomer geometry. Their porous, water-interacting properties will be characterized via water vapor sorption and inverse gas chromatography, inspired by Kitagawa's work on soft porous crystals. Photocatalytic hydrogen evolution rates will be measured under visible light and correlated with spectroscopic and computational analyses of the density of electronic states and binding energy informatics for key reaction intermediates.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a fundamental structure-activity relationship linking engineered amorphousness to catalytic function in metal-free polymers. It aims to deliver a new design paradigm for platinum-free photocatalysts and expand the conceptual toolkit of porous materials science by applying principles of dynamic softness and defect engineering to catalytic organic matrices.
proposals.novelty: While disorder is typically considered detrimental in photocatalysis, we propose to use it as a primary design parameter to create novel active sites, fusing concepts from soft porous crystal recognition with the challenge of replacing precious metal co-catalysts.

proposals.title: Bio-Inspired Hierarchical Metal-Organic Frameworks for Compartmentalized Cascade Catalysis in Direct Methanol Fuel Cells
proposals.motivation: Direct methanol fuel cells (DMFCs) suffer from performance limitations due to slow reaction kinetics, catalyst poisoning, and crossover of methanol to the cathode. Current composite catalysts often lack the precise spatial organization needed to manage these coupled reactions efficiently, leading to diffusion limitations and suboptimal local environments that degrade activity and durability.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that constructing a composite catalyst with a bio-inspired, hierarchically porous architecture that physically compartmentalizes distinct catalytic sites for methanol oxidation (MOR) and oxygen reduction (ORR) will create synergistic microenvironments, enabling substrate channeling and protection of active components to significantly enhance overall catalytic performance and stability.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a core-shell hierarchical porous material using Kitagawa's expertise in soft porous crystals and ultramicroporous materials. A water-stable, ultramicroporous MOF core (e.g., a prototypal diamondoid topology material) will be designed to host and protect ORR catalysts (e.g., Pt nanoparticles). A mesoporous MOF or MOF-derived carbon shell, grown epitaxially on the core, will compartmentalize MOR catalysts (e.g., PtRu alloys). The synthesis will employ dynamic sorption and high-temperature calcination under controlled atmospheres to fine-tune porosity and integrate metal/metal oxide nanoparticles. The architecture will be characterized by in-situ X-ray diffraction and gas/vapor sorption, while catalytic performance will be evaluated in a custom fuel cell testing setup.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a new design principle for fuel cell catalysts based on bio-mimetic compartmentalization within hierarchical MOFs. It will provide fundamental insights into how engineered porosity and spatial organization of active sites govern cascade reaction efficiency, moving beyond simple material mixing. The resulting composite material aims to demonstrate a measurable leap in DMFC power density and long-term stability.
proposals.novelty: While hierarchical porosity is known, its application to physically separate and synergistically couple two distinct fuel cell reactions within a single, designed MOF-based particle is novel. This approach directly transposes the concept of enzymatic substrate channeling to heterogeneous electrocatalysis, a non-obvious adaptation from biocatalysis.

proposals.title: Bio-Inspired Hierarchical Porous Catalysts: Organic-Templated Core-Shell Earth-Abundant Metal Composites for Efficient Water Splitting
proposals.motivation: The high cost and scarcity of noble metals like platinum limit the scalability of efficient catalysts for critical energy reactions such as water splitting. Drawing inspiration from biomineralization, where minimal organic components template robust inorganic composites, offers a promising path to replace precious metals. This project aims to create high-performance, low-cost catalysts by architecting earth-abundant metals (e.g., Fe, Co, Ni) and their oxides into hierarchical porous structures using organic templates, directly addressing the need for sustainable and economical catalytic materials.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that sacrificial or conductive porous organic polymers can template the formation of hierarchically porous core-shell nanostructures from earth-abundant metals, leading to composites with enhanced mass transport, electron conductivity, and catalytic stability that rival noble-metal-based systems.
proposals.method: We will synthesize soft porous crystalline coordination polymers or covalent organic frameworks (COFs) with nitro or other functional groups to act as programmable sacrificial templates. These organic matrices will be infiltrated with precursors of earth-abundant metals (e.g., Ni, Co) and oxides via solution impregnation. Subsequent controlled pyrolysis or chemical etching will remove the template or convert it to a conductive carbonaceous phase, yielding a hierarchically porous metal/metal-oxide composite with core-shell motifs. The materials will be characterized by in-situ X-ray diffraction and dynamic water vapor sorption to study structural evolution and porosity, with catalytic performance evaluated for the oxygen evolution reaction (OER) at elevated temperatures.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a generalizable, bio-inspired synthesis strategy for low-cost, hierarchically porous composite catalysts. It will provide fundamental insights into the role of organic templates in controlling the assembly and electronic structure of earth-abundant catalytic cores. The resulting materials are expected to demonstrate benchmark activity and stability for energy-relevant reactions, contributing a new material design paradigm beyond conventional noble-metal catalysts.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in merging the concepts of soft porous crystal-programmed assembly (from Kitagawa's work on hydrocarbon separation) with biomineralization-inspired hierarchical structuring to create catalytic composites, rather than solely for sorption. This approach uniquely uses the organic component not just as a sacrificial template but potentially as an in-situ-formed conductive matrix to enhance electron transfer within the composite architecture.

proposals.title: Kinetically Trapped, MOF-Mimetic Ceramic Composites via Computational Inverse Design for High-Temperature Oxidative Desulfurization
proposals.motivation: Metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) offer unparalleled designability for catalysis but suffer from limited thermal/chemical stability, precluding their use in demanding processes like high-temperature oxidative desulfurization of fuels. While robust composites exist, they lack the precise, atomically defined active-site placement achievable in MOFs. A method to translate a MOF's optimal spatial architecture into a durable, non-labile material is a critical unmet challenge.
proposals.hypothesis: That a computationally derived, optimal pore/site architecture for a target reaction can be physically realized and kinetically trapped via irreversible sequential reactions on a sacrificial scaffold, yielding a composite with MOF-like spatial precision and ceramic-like robustness.
proposals.method: We will employ genetic algorithm-based inverse design, using binding energy and transition-state descriptors from density functional theory (DFT) to evolve a digital 'blueprint' MOF for thiophene oxidation. This blueprint will guide the atomic layer deposition (ALD) of metal oxides (e.g., ZrO2, WO3) onto a 3D-printed polymeric template with complementary topology. Subsequent high-temperature calcination will cross-link the oxide network and remove the template, kinetically trapping the designed nanoarchitecture into a stable, amorphous ceramic composite. Dynamic water vapor sorption and in-situ X-ray diffraction will monitor structural evolution and stability.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel paradigm for creating 'MOF-inspired' robust catalysts, moving beyond the synthesis of the MOF itself. It will deliver a new class of materials combining computational design precision with experimental durability, specifically validated for a high-temperature catalytic application previously inaccessible to porous coordination polymers.
proposals.novelty: The approach is distinct from synthesizing MOF-composite hybrids; it uses the MOF as a purely computational intermediate to guide the irreversible assembly of a completely different, stable material class, achieving spatial control typically lost in conventional ceramic synthesis.

proposals.title: Programmable Water-Resilient Catalysis via MOF-Templated Composite Architectures
proposals.motivation: Heterogeneous catalysts often suffer from deactivation in humid or aqueous environments, limiting their practical application in green chemistry and energy conversion. While Metal-Organic Frameworks (MOFs) offer exquisite control over pore environment and hydrophilicity/hydrophobicity, their direct use as high-temperature catalysts is constrained by thermal and chemical stability. There is a critical need to translate the precise, function-oriented design principles of MOFs into robust, composite catalytic materials that retain activity in the presence of water.
proposals.hypothesis: By using hydrolytically stable, ultramicroporous MOFs as sacrificial templates or structural guides, we can synthesize composite metal oxide or metal@oxide catalysts with pre-programmed pore architectures and surface functionalities that confer both high activity and exceptional water vapor resilience during thermocatalytic reactions.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a series of soft porous crystals and hybrid ultramicroporous materials (HUMs) with nitro or other polar functional groups, known to govern water sorption dynamics, as structural templates. These MOFs will be infiltrated with metal precursors (e.g., Co, Ni, Cu) and subjected to controlled pyrolysis under inert atmosphere, a method used for MOF-derived chemiresistive sensors. The pyrolysis conditions (high temperature profile, atmosphere) will be tuned to decompose the organic framework while preserving the inorganic metal clusters and the topological pore structure, resulting in a composite of metal/metal oxide nanoparticles within a porous carbon or oxide matrix. The water vapor sorption properties and dynamic structural changes of the parent MOFs will inform the selection of templates for target reactions like PROX or wet CO2 hydrogenation.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel materials design paradigm, moving beyond MOFs-as-catalysts to MOFs-as-blueprints, for creating structured composites. It will deliver a new class of water-tolerant catalysts and provide fundamental insights into how inherited pore chemistry from a MOF precursor influences reactant and water molecule transport at high temperature.
proposals.novelty: The approach is distinct from simple MOF pyrolysis or MOF/oxide blending; it explicitly uses the dynamic, programable host-guest chemistry of soft porous crystals—particularly their water sorption behavior—to dictate the final composite's active site environment and pore architecture, a strategy not yet explored for composite catalyst design.

proposals.title: High-Throughput Synthesis and Screening of Amorphous-Crystalline Metal-Organic Composites for Enhanced Photocatalytic Water Splitting
proposals.motivation: Photocatalytic water splitting is a promising route for sustainable hydrogen production, but efficiency is often limited by poor charge carrier separation and slow surface reaction kinetics. While crystalline porous materials like MOFs offer structural precision, their rigid frameworks can restrict active site accessibility. Conversely, amorphous phases provide structural flexibility and abundant defects but typically lack long-range order for efficient charge transport. A systematic investigation of composites combining these phases is lacking.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the intentional integration of amorphous polymer domains, sharing the same metal-linker chemistry as a crystalline metal-organic framework (MOF) matrix, will create synergistic interfaces that enhance photocatalytic activity by improving charge separation and providing tailored adsorption sites for water molecules.
proposals.method: We will employ a high-throughput, combinatorial robotic platform to synthesize libraries of composites by modulating the synthesis conditions (e.g., modulator concentration, temperature) of prototypical water-stable MOFs like MIL-125(Ti)-NH2 or UiO-66(Zr) to introduce controlled amorphous domains. A custom automated screening system will then characterize the libraries using parallel photoluminescence spectroscopy (for charge recombination) and a colorimetric assay for photocatalytic hydrogen evolution under simulated solar light. Data will be analyzed using multivariate regression and machine learning (e.g., random forest algorithms) to identify key compositional and structural descriptors governing performance.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a data-driven platform for the rational design of non-traditional composite photocatalysts where amorphicity is a functional element. It will generate a foundational structure-activity map for amorphous-crystalline composites in photocatalysis, directly linking synthetic parameters to photophysical properties and catalytic output for water splitting.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in applying high-throughput drug discovery methodologies to systematically engineer and optimize amorphous-crystalline interfaces within isochemical metal-organic systems, treating the amorphous phase not as an impurity but as a deliberately tunable component for enhanced photoactivity.

proposals.title: Feedback-Driven Synthesis: A Machine Learning and Dynamic Combinatorial Chemistry Approach to Self-Optimizing Covalent Organic Framework Catalysts for CO2 Reduction
proposals.motivation: The discovery of efficient, stable catalysts for CO2 reduction is a critical challenge in sustainable chemistry. While covalent organic frameworks (COFs) offer a promising, tunable platform, their rational design for complex, multi-step catalysis remains difficult due to the vast combinatorial space of building blocks and the static nature of conventional synthesis. An integrated approach that dynamically evolves the catalyst under operational conditions could dramatically accelerate discovery.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a closed-loop system combining machine learning (ML) for initial prediction and dynamic combinatorial chemistry (DCC) for experimental evolution will drive the self-assembly and enrichment of COF structures with superior catalytic performance for CO2 reduction, surpassing the capabilities of static design or screening alone.
proposals.method: We will first train a graph neural network (GNN) model on pre-2026 databases (e.g., the Cambridge Structural Database, CoRE MOF) to predict COF stability and properties. This model will design an initial library of amine and aldehyde building blocks with reversible imine linkages. This library will be subjected to DCC screening under simulated CO2 reduction conditions (e.g., electrochemical, with water vapor present), where the dynamic covalent bonds allow the system to reconfigure. High-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) and mass spectrometry will provide real-time compositional data, which will be used as feedback to retrain and refine the GNN model in an iterative loop, guiding subsequent DCC rounds.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel, adaptive methodology for the discovery of functional porous materials, moving beyond static prediction to systems that evolve towards a target function. It will yield new, high-performance COF catalysts for CO2 reduction and provide a generalizable platform for optimizing complex, multi-component porous solids under dynamic conditions.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the synergistic, closed-loop integration of ML and DCC specifically for COF catalyst optimization, creating a self-improving system where experimental outcomes directly and iteratively inform the computational model, a concept not yet realized in porous materials synthesis by 2025.

proposals.title: Programmable Assembly of Heteronuclear Single-Atom Catalysts via DNA Origami Templating for Enhanced Water-Splitting Electrocatalysis
proposals.motivation: Single-atom catalysts (SACs) offer exceptional atom efficiency, but rationally constructing composite SACs with precise spatial arrangement of different metal atoms remains a formidable synthetic challenge. This precision is crucial for unlocking synergistic effects in multi-step reactions, such as the oxygen evolution reaction (OER), which is a bottleneck in water splitting. Pre-2026, MOFs have been used to host single atoms, but lack the programmability to dictate exact heteronuclear geometries.
proposals.hypothesis: That DNA origami nanostructures can serve as programmable scaffolds to position distinct metal-binding sites with sub-nanometer precision, enabling the synthesis of stable, heteronuclear single-atom composite catalysts with tailored electronic structures and superior catalytic activity for water oxidation.
proposals.method: We will design 2D DNA origami tiles with specific aptamer sequences positioned to bind Pt(II) and Co(II) ions at defined distances (e.g., 0.5 nm, 1.0 nm). These metallated DNA scaffolds will be immobilized on conductive supports like reduced graphene oxide. A subsequent low-temperature pyrolysis step (250-400°C) under inert atmosphere will carbonize the DNA, transforming it into a nitrogen-doped carbon matrix that entraps and stabilizes the Pt-Co single-atom pairs. Advanced characterization (AC-HAADF-STEM, XAS) will confirm atomic dispersion and structure.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a general, bio-inspired pathway for the rational design of multi-metallic SACs with controlled atomic architecture. It will provide fundamental insights into how the spatial and electronic coupling between two different single atoms governs catalytic synergy, specifically for OER. The method bridges the fields of structural nanotechnology and heterogeneous catalysis.
proposals.novelty: While DNA origami has been used to organize nanoparticles, its application as a sacrificial, geometrically precise template for synthesizing *atomically dispersed* bimetallic active sites is unprecedented. It directly addresses the 'positional control' problem in SAC design that traditional porous material synthesis cannot solve.

proposals.title: Template-Directed Irreversible Synthesis of Hydrolytically Stable Covalent Organic Frameworks via Orthogonal Metal-Organic Coordination
proposals.motivation: The hydrolytic instability of many crystalline covalent organic frameworks (COFs), stemming from the reversible nature of their imine or boronate ester linkages, severely limits their practical application in aqueous or humid environments, including water sorption and separation. While irreversible linkages (e.g., amides, olefins) yield robust COFs, their synthesis often lacks the error-correction needed for high crystallinity. A method to decouple crystallization control from final bond stability is a critical, unmet challenge in porous materials chemistry.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a labile, metal-organic coordination framework can act as a supramolecular template to pre-organize organic building blocks, guiding the irreversible formation of a hydrolytically stable, multi-component COF with long-range order, after which the metal template can be selectively removed.
proposals.method: We will design a multi-component system where organic linkers functionalized with both irreversible coupling groups (e.g., anhydrides for amide formation) and orthogonal, reversible coordination sites (e.g., pyridyl, carboxylate) are combined with labile metal ions (e.g., Zn²⁺, Cd²⁺). The metal-ligand coordination will first drive the assembly of a crystalline, dynamic metal-organic scaffold under solvothermal conditions. Subsequently, the irreversible covalent coupling between the pre-organized linkers will be thermally activated, locking in the COF structure. Finally, the metal-ion template will be selectively removed via competitive complexation, yielding a purely organic, crystalline COF with robust, non-reversible linkages.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a general template-guided synthesis paradigm for COFs, directly producing materials with enhanced stability for applications in water vapor sorption and humid gas separation. It will demonstrate the first integration of dynamic metal-organic chemistry to direct irreversible covalent assembly, expanding the toolbox for creating complex, multi-component porous organic materials.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the orthogonal use of reversible metal-ligand coordination solely as a transient templating mechanism to achieve crystallinity in a COF with inherently irreversible, and thus highly stable, primary covalent bonds, moving beyond the reliance on the reversibility of the final framework linkages for structural ordering.

proposals.title: Inverse Design of Fuel Cell Electrodes via Metastable COF Composites: Breaking the Reversibility Dogma for Enhanced Proton Transport
proposals.motivation: Proton-exchange membrane fuel cells (PEMFCs) require electrodes that integrate high proton conductivity, catalyst dispersion, and stability. Conventional, fully reversible COF synthesis yields thermodynamically stable structures, limiting access to non-equilibrium phases with potentially superior transport properties. Adapting catalysts to standard electrode architectures creates performance bottlenecks; a paradigm shift is needed where the electrode is custom-designed around uniquely advantageous material properties.
proposals.hypothesis: Strategically introducing irreversible covalent steps during COF synthesis will create metastable, multi-component composites with finely tuned, non-equilibrium proton conduction pathways and catalyst anchoring sites. These composite properties can serve as fixed targets for an inverse-design computational framework to fabricate optimized, hierarchical electrode architectures.
proposals.method: We will synthesize COF composites using irreversible imide or amide linkages, combining sulfonated and catalytically active (e.g., porphyrin-based) building blocks under kinetic control to create metastable domain structures. Their water vapor sorption, proton conductivity, and stability will be characterized at high temperature and humidity. These experimental properties become constraints for an inverse problem solved via a genetic algorithm, which designs 3D electrode microstructures (pore networks, catalyst/ionomer gradients) that maximize performance metrics (current density) when simulated with a continuum transport model.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel materials-by-design pipeline that links non-equilibrium COF synthesis directly to device optimization. It will yield new fundamental understanding of proton transport in metastable porous organic frameworks and demonstrate a proof-of-concept electrode with performance exceeding those based on conventional, reversible COFs.
proposals.novelty: It directly challenges the reversibility dogma in COF synthesis to access non-equilibrium properties and couples this to an inverse-design paradigm for fuel cells—treating the synthesized composite as a fixed constraint rather than a variable to be fitted into a standard electrode.

proposals.title: Scalable Synthesis of Water-Stable, Porous Metal-Organic Composite Catalysts for Continuous-Flow Multi-Component Reactions
proposals.motivation: Multi-component reactions (MCRs) are powerful for synthesizing complex molecules, but their industrial adoption is hindered by a lack of robust, reusable, and scalable heterogeneous catalysts compatible with diverse reagent pools. Pre-2026, Kitagawa's work on water-stable, ultramicroporous materials and dynamic sorption properties provides a unique foundation to address catalyst stability and substrate selectivity in demanding reaction environments. Integrating these porous platforms with scalable manufacturing is critical to transition MCRs from batch discovery to continuous production.
proposals.hypothesis: That porous metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) or hybrid ultramicroporous materials (HUMs), engineered for hydrothermal stability and functionalized with organocatalytic sites via scalable impregnation, will provide a versatile, reusable platform for catalyzing diverse MCRs beyond classical models, with performance dictated by the host's pore chemistry and the guest's dynamic interaction with substrates.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a library of Zr-, or Fe-based MOFs and HUMs known for water/thermal stability (e.g., analogues of prototypal diamondoid topology materials). Organocatalytic motifs (e.g., proline derivatives, bifunctional acid-base sites) will be incorporated via continuous flow impregnation or spray drying, leveraging Kitagawa's expertise in sorption dynamics. These composites will be tested in continuous-flow reactors for new MCRs involving ketones, heterocyclic amines, and activated olefins. Operando spectroscopic and sorption analysis will correlate pore environment dynamics, substrate recognition (inspired by inverse hydrocarbon recognition concepts), and catalytic efficiency.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a scalable manufacturing protocol for robust MOF-organocatalyst composites, directly addressing the catalyst supply bottleneck. It will establish structure-property guidelines for porous hosts in MCR catalysis and demonstrate several new, practical continuous-flow MCR pathways, accelerating the translation of MCR chemistry to process-relevant applications.
proposals.novelty: The fusion of Kitagawa's fundamental research on water-stable porous systems and dynamic sorption with scalable catalyst manufacturing and continuous-flow reaction engineering is novel. It moves beyond using MOFs as static supports to actively employ their programmed pore dynamics and recognition properties to control multi-component reaction sequences and selectivity.

proposals.title: Decoupling Crystallinity and Linker Chemistry in Conjugated Polymers: A Systematic Study of Butadiene Analogs for Photocatalytic Water Splitting
proposals.motivation: Conjugated polymers (CPs) are promising metal-free photocatalysts for hydrogen evolution, but their structure-property relationships are poorly understood. Recent reports suggest buta-1,3-diene linkages offer superior performance, yet it is unclear if this is due to intrinsic electronic structure or the crystalline order it often promotes. Resolving this is critical for designing efficient, solution-processable, and potentially amorphous photocatalysts that combine high activity with ease of fabrication.
proposals.hypothesis: The photocatalytic superiority of the butadiene linkage in conjugated polymers is not an intrinsic electronic effect but is contingent upon achieving long-range crystalline order, meaning that amorphous polymers with optimized linkage chemistry can achieve comparable performance.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a 2D matrix of polymers by systematically varying the conjugated linker (butadiene, vinylene, phenylene-vinylene, acetylene) and the degree of crystallinity, controlled via side-chain engineering and polymerization conditions. Photocatalytic hydrogen evolution rates will be measured under visible light using triethanolamine as a sacrificial agent. Performance will be correlated with structural data (PXRD, PDF analysis for local order), optoelectronic properties (UV-vis, PL, TRPL), and charge carrier dynamics (TRMC, electrochemical impedance spectroscopy).
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a fundamental, decoupled understanding of how crystallinity and linker chemistry independently influence photocatalytic activity in CPs. It will provide clear design rules for creating high-performance, disordered photocatalysts, potentially expanding the materials palette for scalable solar fuel production.
proposals.novelty: Moving beyond binary comparisons, this study employs a systematic two-dimensional design space (crystallinity x linkage) to uniquely isolate the true driver of performance, a strategy not yet applied in the CP photocatalysis field pre-2026.

proposals.title: Probing the Role of Long-Range Order: Photocatalytic Water Splitting in Amorphous Coordination Polymers with Identical Metal-Organic Linkages
proposals.motivation: The exceptional photocatalytic performance of crystalline metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) is often attributed to their long-range periodic order, which facilitates charge carrier transport. However, this paradigm overlooks the potential of amorphous coordination polymers, which possess the same primary coordination bonds but lack crystallinity. Given Kitagawa's expertise in porous and water-interactive materials, exploring this amorphous-crystalline dichotomy is crucial to fundamentally understand the structural prerequisites for photoactivity, potentially unlocking new design principles for robust, processable photocatalysts.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that amorphous coordination polymers, constructed from the same photoactive metal clusters and organic linkers as their crystalline MOF counterparts, can retain significant photocatalytic activity for water splitting, challenging the notion that long-range crystallinity is an absolute requirement.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a series of isostructural pairs: a crystalline MOF (e.g., a UiO-66 or MIL-125 analogue with Ti/Zr nodes and functionalized terephthalate linkers) and its amorphous polymer analogue via rapid precipitation or mechanochemical methods, ensuring identical local metal-linker coordination. Their structures will be characterized by PXRD, EXAFS, and solid-state NMR. High-temperature (up to 150°C) water vapor sorption analysis will probe hydrophilicity and pore accessibility. Photocatalytic hydrogen evolution from water will be measured under UV/visible light using a dynamic gas sorption apparatus coupled with mass spectrometry, with triethanolamine as a sacrificial agent.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a direct structure-activity relationship separating the effects of local coordination chemistry from long-range order in metal-organic photocatalysts. It will either validate crystallinity as mandatory for high performance or reveal that amorphous polymers are viable, potentially more synthetically flexible, alternatives, expanding the toolkit for designing functional porous materials for solar energy conversion.
proposals.novelty: While amorphous MOFs have been studied for gas sorption, their systematic evaluation for photocatalytic water splitting, directly compared to isochemical crystalline frameworks, is a novel and non-obvious approach to decouple the roles of short- and long-range structure in photo-driven processes.

proposals.title: One-Pot Synthesis of Heterolinkage Covalent Organic Frameworks via Multi-Component Dynamic Covalent Chemistry for Tailored Water Sorption
proposals.motivation: Covalent Organic Frameworks (COFs) are typically constructed from one or two types of building blocks forming a single, homogeneous linkage, limiting structural diversity and property fine-tuning. Pre-2026, Kitagawa's work on soft porous crystals and ultramicroporous materials has demonstrated that subtle structural variations, especially in water-material interactions, lead to significant functional differences in sorption and separation. A synthetic strategy to systematically incorporate multiple, distinct linkages into a single COF lattice could unlock unprecedented control over pore chemistry and framework dynamics, crucial for advanced applications in atmospheric water harvesting or hydrocarbon purification.
proposals.hypothesis: By employing three or more judiciously chosen molecular precursors that undergo two or more compatible, reversible reactions (e.g., imine condensation and boronate ester formation) in a single pot, we can synthesize crystalline COFs with heterogeneous, interwoven linkages, and that the nature and ratio of these linkages will dictate the framework's hydrophilicity, flexibility, and sorption profile for water vapor.
proposals.method: We will design multi-component reactions using aldehydes, amines, and boronic acids/boroxine-forming precursors. Reactions will be conducted solvothermally, with the temperature and solvent composition dynamically modulated to balance the kinetics and reversibility of the different covalent bond formations. The resulting materials will be characterized by in-situ PXRD, solid-state NMR, and gas/vapor sorption analysis (especially water isotherms) to confirm crystallinity, linkage composition, and porosity. High-throughput screening of precursor combinations will be guided by principles of dynamic combinatorial chemistry.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a generalizable, one-pot synthetic methodology for creating heterolinkage COFs, a new subclass of porous materials. It will provide fundamental insights into how competing dynamic covalent reactions can be orchestrated to form ordered frameworks. The resulting materials are expected to exhibit unique, tunable water sorption properties, advancing the design of responsive porous materials for environmental applications.
proposals.novelty: While multi-component reactions are known in molecular synthesis and some dual-linkage COFs exist, the deliberate, simultaneous use of three or more components to form two distinct, co-existing covalent linkages in a single crystalline COF lattice is unprecedented. This moves beyond post-synthetic modification or mixed-linker approaches to achieve intrinsic chemical heterogeneity via controlled synthesis.

proposals.title: Feedback-Optimized Dynamic Combinatorial Chemistry for the Synthesis of Kinetically Trapped Covalent Organic Frameworks with Enhanced Porosity and Water Stability
proposals.motivation: Covalent Organic Frameworks (COFs) often form as kinetically trapped, amorphous, or poorly crystalline phases, limiting their porosity and stability, especially in the presence of water. Pre-2026, dynamic combinatorial chemistry (DCC) has been used to discover molecular complexes, but its application to steer the synthesis of extended porous solids like COFs remains unexplored. This gap prevents the rational targeting of COFs with optimal properties for applications such as water vapor sorption or hydrocarbon separation.
proposals.hypothesis: Integrating real-time monitoring of a dynamic combinatorial library (DCL) with a closed-loop feedback system that adjusts reaction parameters can actively steer the synthesis away from the thermodynamic sink and toward the kinetic pathways that yield COFs with high crystallinity, porosity, and hydrolytic stability.
proposals.method: We will construct a DCL from aldehyde and amine building blocks in reversible Schiff-base chemistry, monitored in-situ by HPLC for speciation and synchrotron PXRD for crystallinity. A feedback loop, governed by a multi-objective optimization algorithm (e.g., Bayesian optimization), will process this data to dynamically adjust reaction parameters such as temperature, catalyst concentration, and water activity. This system will iteratively perturb the DCL equilibrium to favor the assembly of building blocks that correlate with the target material properties, moving beyond the thermodynamically most stable molecular complex.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel synthetic paradigm for porous materials, demonstrating the first closed-loop, feedback-driven synthesis of COFs. It will produce new, kinetically trapped COF phases with enhanced crystallinity and water stability, directly relevant to Kitagawa's themes of water-stable porous materials. The methodology will provide a general platform for the targeted discovery of functional porous materials beyond the limitations of thermodynamic control.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the real-time, algorithmic coupling of DCL analysis with the evolving solid-state properties of a forming framework, creating an active learning loop for materials synthesis. This moves DCC from a discovery tool for molecular hosts to a directed method for synthesizing extended porous solids with pre-defined characteristics.

proposals.title: Autonomous Discovery of Hydrolytically Stable Covalent Organic Frameworks via a Closed-Loop Robotic Platform with Adaptive AI Guidance
proposals.motivation: The discovery of covalent organic frameworks (COFs) with high crystallinity, permanent porosity, and hydrolytic stability remains a significant challenge, limiting their practical application in areas like water vapor sorption and gas separation. Traditional synthesis is slow and often yields poorly crystalline or non-porous materials, creating a vast, inefficient search through chemical and reaction condition space. An autonomous system that rapidly synthesizes, validates, and learns from each experiment is urgently needed to navigate this complexity and identify robust, functional materials.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a closed-loop autonomous platform integrating robotic synthesis with real-time in-line characterization and an adaptive AI controller can dramatically accelerate the discovery of hydrolytically stable, porous COFs by efficiently mapping the relationship between synthetic parameters (e.g., linker functionality, solvent, temperature) and the resulting material's crystallinity and porosity.
proposals.method: A high-throughput robotic synthesizer will execute solvothermal reactions for COF formation, with a focus on imine and hydrazone linkages known for potential stability. The reaction products will be automatically transferred to integrated characterization modules: in-line powder X-ray diffraction (PXRD) for crystallinity and phase identification, and a microbalance for immediate N2 or H2O vapor sorption analysis at 77K or 298K, respectively. An AI controller, using a Bayesian optimization algorithm informed by pre-2026 Gaussian Process models, will analyze the real-time PXRD and sorption data to predict promising, unexplored reaction conditions, dynamically updating the experimental queue to maximize the discovery of crystalline, porous, and water-stable materials.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a fully autonomous materials discovery platform specifically for porous organic frameworks, generating a validated dataset of synthesis-structure-property relationships for COFs. It will yield novel, hydrolytically stable COF candidates for vapor sorption applications, directly extending Kitagawa's expertise in water-porous materials. The closed-loop methodology represents a paradigm shift from high-throughput trial-and-error to intelligent, feedback-driven exploration.
proposals.novelty: While robotic synthesis and AI for materials exist separately, their tight integration into a single, physically closed loop with real-time, automated crystallinity and porosity validation for COFs is unprecedented. The system's ability to use immediate sorption data (especially for water vapor) as a primary feedback signal for adaptive experimental design is a novel, application-driven advancement beyond standard composition-focused searches.

proposals.title: Dynamic Interfacial Vitrimer Grafting for Processable, Crystalline Covalent Organic Framework Monoliths with Enhanced Water Vapor Sorption Properties
proposals.motivation: Covalent Organic Frameworks (COFs) offer exceptional porosity and chemical tunability for applications like water vapor sorption and hydrocarbon separation, as seen in Kitagawa's work on ultramicroporous materials. However, their crystalline, insoluble nature renders them as intractable powders, severely limiting their processability into practical, shaped devices or membranes. Integrating the intrinsic functionality of crystalline frameworks with macroscopic processability remains a critical, unsolved challenge in porous materials science.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that chemically grafting a thin, compliant layer of a dynamic covalent vitrimer network onto pre-formed COF crystallites will create an adaptive interfacial phase that enables macroscopic flow and reshaping under heat while preserving the crystallinity, porosity, and chemical functionality of the individual COF domains.
proposals.method: We will synthesize crystalline, imine- or hydrazone-linked COF particles with surface-exposed functional groups (e.g., -OH, -NH2). A thin vitrimer network, based on dynamic transesterification bonds in a epoxy-acid system, will be grafted onto these surfaces via in-situ polymerization with compatible linkers. The composite will be characterized by PXRD, N2/H2O sorption, and electron microscopy. Processability will be tested by hot-pressing powders into monolithic discs, followed by analysis of their mechanical integrity, recyclability via re-pressing, and retention of sorption properties, particularly for water vapor.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel materials design principle for creating processable crystalline composites, directly addressing a major bottleneck in COF utilization. It will yield a new class of shaped porous monoliths that retain the high performance of parent COFs for vapor sorption and recognition, enabling their practical deployment in adsorption-based technologies.
proposals.novelty: While vitrimers and COFs are known separately, the strategy of using a vitrimer not as a bulk matrix but as a thin, grafted interfacial 'glue' specifically to enable plasticity in a crystalline porous solid is novel. This approach maintains high crystallinity and accessibility, unlike conventional polymer composites where crystallinity is often sacrificed.

proposals.title: Engineering Covalent Alloys: A Phase Diagram Approach to Predict Topological Freezing in Multi-Component COF Vitrimers
proposals.motivation: Covalent Organic Framework (COF) vitrimers, which combine crystallinity with dynamic covalent bonds, offer transformative potential for processable, self-healing porous materials. However, pre-2026 research lacks a predictive framework to manage the complex interplay between multiple dynamic linkages (e.g., boronic esters, imines), their exchange kinetics, and the resulting viscoelastic properties, hindering controlled processing and property tuning.
proposals.hypothesis: The dynamic covalent network of a multi-component COF vitrimer can be treated as a 'covalent alloy,' where its topological freezing transition and viscoelastic spectrum can be predictively mapped and controlled using principles analogous to metallurgical phase diagrams, linking composition and thermal history to network properties.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a series of multi-component 2D COFs incorporating varying molar ratios of boronic ester and imine linkages. Using high-temperature dynamic mechanical analysis and in-situ variable-temperature PXRD, we will construct empirical phase diagrams plotting composition and temperature against storage modulus and crystalline domain size. These diagrams will be correlated with exchange kinetics measured via stress-relaxation experiments to establish predictive models for topological freezing temperatures and annealing conditions.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish the first quantitative 'composition-processing-property' framework for dynamic COFs, directly enabling the rational design of materials with tailored processability and stability. It will introduce the powerful conceptual tool of phase diagrams from metallurgy to the field of crystalline porous polymers, advancing their application in membranes and sensors requiring reprocessability.
proposals.novelty: The non-obvious adaptation of metallurgical phase diagram concepts to map dynamic covalent chemistry in crystalline frameworks represents a significant conceptual leap beyond the trial-and-error approaches common in pre-2026 vitrimer research, treating the covalent network as an engineerable alloy.

proposals.title: Mechanically Reinforced, Free-Standing Covalent Organic Framework Monolayers via Polymer-Crosslinked 2D Templating for Durable Water Vapor Sorption
proposals.motivation: Two-dimensional covalent organic frameworks (2D COFs) offer immense potential for gas sorption and catalysis, but their practical application is hindered by the fragility of exfoliated monolayers and the restacking of nanosheets, which drastically reduces accessible surface area. Pre-2026, achieving robust, free-standing COF monolayers with preserved porosity for direct device integration remains a significant materials challenge, particularly for applications like selective water vapor capture where dynamic structural stability is crucial.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a surface-confined synthesis of a monolayer COF on a 2D crystalline template (e.g., graphene oxide), followed by in-situ infiltration and covalent cross-linking of a flexible polymer network to the COF's structural nodes, will yield a mechanically reinforced hybrid nanosheet that maintains high porosity and resists restacking after template removal.
proposals.method: We will first synthesize a monolayer of a water-stable, imine-linked COF via dynamic covalent chemistry on a graphene oxide template. Subsequently, we will infiltrate the COF pores with a difunctional monomer (e.g., a diisocyanate) and a flexible diol, initiating a polyaddition reaction to form a polyurethane network that covalently bonds to the COF's amine nodes. The graphene oxide template will be etched away using a mild chemical or thermal treatment, leaving a free-standing, polymer-crosslinked COF (PC-COF) nanosheet. Characterization will involve in-situ grazing-incidence X-ray diffraction, atomic force microscopy-based nanoindentation, and dynamic water vapor sorption analysis.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a generalizable templating and reinforcement strategy to produce durable, free-standing COF monolayers, directly addressing the mechanical instability that limits their device integration. It will contribute to the field of hybrid ultramicroporous materials by creating a new class of soft yet robust crystalline sheets with tunable sorption dynamics, specifically for water vapor management applications relevant to Kitagawa's research themes.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the synergistic, covalent integration of a flexible polymer mesh within a rigid 2D COF lattice during synthesis, which is distinct from simple blending or layer-by-layer assembly, to create a monolithic hybrid nanosheet with engineered mechanical properties without sacrificing crystallinity or microporosity.

proposals.title: Dynamic Water-Mediated Assembly of Hierarchical COF Nanosheet Composites for Tunable Proton Transport and Catalysis
proposals.motivation: Proton-exchange membrane fuel cells (PEMFCs) require materials that can manage water and proton transport under dynamic operating conditions, a persistent challenge for conventional porous solids. Covalent Organic Frameworks (COFs) offer precise pore environments, but their integration into functional, hierarchical composites for fuel cells remains underexplored. This research addresses the need for catalysts and membranes where the local chemical environment around active sites can be systematically tuned in response to humidity and temperature.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the assembly of monolayer COF nanosheets, using water-mediated interactions and intercalation chemistry, will create hierarchical composites with dynamically tunable interlayer spacing and pore environments, leading to superior and adaptable proton conductivity and catalytic activity for fuel cell reactions.
proposals.method: We will synthesize imine- or hydrazone-linked 2D COF nanosheets via surfactant-assisted interfacial synthesis. These nanosheets will be assembled into hierarchical composites through layer-by-layer deposition and intercalation with hydrophilic polymers (e.g., sulfonated polyether ether ketone) or metal-organic polyhedra. The interlayer environment will be tuned by controlling the humidity during assembly, exploiting water as a structural template. The composites will be characterized by in-situ variable-humidity XRD and FT-IR to monitor structural dynamics, and their proton conductivity and electrocatalytic performance for the oxygen reduction reaction will be evaluated under controlled temperature and humidity.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new methodology for creating dynamically responsive COF-based composites, directly linking tunable nanoscale architecture to macroscopic proton transport and catalytic properties. It will provide fundamental insights into how water organizes within hierarchical porous materials, advancing the design of next-generation fuel cell components. The project will yield a new class of soft porous crystal-inspired composites with programmable functionality.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in using water as a dynamic template and mediator for the hierarchical assembly of COF nanosheets, creating composites where the interlayer chemistry and spacing are tunable in-situ, a concept inspired by Kitagawa's work on soft porous crystals but extended to 2D covalent frameworks for electrochemical applications.

proposals.title: Amorphous Coordination Polymers with Programmed Porosity: Synthesizing Non-Crystalline Analogues of Prototypal MOF Topologies for Enhanced Water Vapor Sorption Dynamics
proposals.motivation: Kitagawa's work on ultramicroporous materials with diamondoid topology shows exceptional water vapor sorption properties, but their crystalline nature can limit processability and mechanical stability under dynamic conditions. An amorphous porous solid with similar local coordination environments could combine the high affinity and capacity of metal-organic frameworks with the robustness and isotropic properties of glasses, opening new avenues for practical humidity control and sensing applications.
proposals.hypothesis: By employing high-temperature solvothermal synthesis with carefully selected, flexible or multi-topic organic linkers, it is possible to create amorphous coordination polymers that preserve the key metal-linker connectivity (e.g., paddle-wheel Cu2 or Zn4O clusters) of prototypal MOF topologies but lack long-range order, resulting in a tunable, mechanically robust porous network.
proposals.method: We will synthesize amorphous analogues by reacting metal salts (e.g., Cu(II), Zn(II)) with organic linkers similar to those in Kitagawa's crystalline systems (e.g., azolate, carboxylate derivatives) under aggressive solvothermal conditions (150-250°C) to promote kinetic trapping of disordered networks. The local structure will be characterized by pair distribution function (PDF) analysis of high-energy X-ray scattering data to confirm retention of target metal-cluster linkages. Porosity and water sorption properties will be evaluated using high-pressure volumetric analyzers and dynamic vapor sorption (DVS) to measure isotherms and kinetics.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a synthetic methodology for 'amorphous MOF analogues' and provide the first structure-property relationships linking local metal-organic connectivity to sorption behavior in a non-crystalline porous coordination polymer. It will contribute a new class of materials that merge the chemical design of MOFs with the practical advantages of amorphous solids for vapor capture.
proposals.novelty: While amorphous metal-organic materials exist, the deliberate design of an amorphous solid that is a structural inverse of a specific, well-defined crystalline MOF topology, aiming to replicate its sorption functionality without crystallinity, is a novel and non-obvious strategy beyond simple disordering of known phases.

proposals.title: Water-Stable Discrete Metal-Organic Cages via Multi-Component Reactions for Selective Vapor Sorption
proposals.motivation: Kitagawa's pioneering work on soft porous crystals and hybrid ultramicroporous materials has demonstrated exceptional dynamic sorption properties, particularly for water vapor. However, extended frameworks can suffer from stability and processability issues. Discrete metal-organic cages (MOCs) offer a solution, providing well-defined cavities with potential for superior host-guest control, but their synthesis often lacks the efficiency and structural diversity of framework assembly.
proposals.hypothesis: Applying the principles of multi-component reactions, used to construct complex extended frameworks, to the synthesis of discrete MOCs will yield a new class of water-stable, molecularly precise porous materials with tunable, dynamic sorption behavior for polar vapors.
proposals.method: We will employ high-throughput solvothermal and one-pot multi-component reactions, combining predesigned ditopic and tritopic organic ligands (e.g., nitro-functionalized linkers for inverse hydrocarbon recognition) with labile metal ions (e.g., Zn²⁺, Cu²⁺) under varied temperature and concentration conditions to favor discrete cage formation over polymerization. Products will be characterized by SCXRD, PXRD, and NMR. Sorption properties, particularly for water and C3 hydrocarbons, will be analyzed using dynamic vapor sorption (DVS) and inverse gas chromatography (IGC) to map binding energies and selectivity.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a robust synthetic paradigm for functional discrete porous materials, bridging the gap between MOFs and molecular hosts. It will deliver fundamental insights into how confinement in a finite, soluble cage affects guest binding dynamics compared to infinite frameworks, potentially enabling new applications in molecular sensing or as additives in composite membranes.
proposals.novelty: While multi-component synthesis is established for MOFs, its deliberate application to arrest growth at the discrete cage level is unexplored. Combining this with Kitagawa's expertise in dynamic, water-interactive systems targets a non-obvious class of materials with molecularly precise porosity.

proposals.title: Probing the Role of Nanoconfinement in Polymer-Induced Local pH Shifts: A Comparative Study Using Soft Porous Crystals and Surface-Adsorbed Systems
proposals.motivation: Local pH modulation within confined spaces is crucial for catalysis, sensing, and biomimetic processes. While polymer-functionalized porous materials like metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) are known to create unique chemical environments, it is unclear whether the observed pH effects are a direct consequence of nanoconfinement or can be replicated by polymers simply adsorbed on open surfaces or free in solution. Resolving this is key to designing next-generation responsive materials for applications like gas purification and chemical detection.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the rigid, size-restrictive pore architecture of soft porous crystals is necessary to generate and sustain the pronounced local pH shifts induced by functional polymers, an effect that will be significantly diminished or absent in surface-adsorbed or free-polymer systems due to lack of spatial constraint and cooperative interactions.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a series of nitro-functionalized, flexible MOFs (e.g., ELM-11 analogs) known for their dynamic pore response, and post-synthetically graft pH-responsive polyelectrolytes (e.g., poly(acrylic acid)) within the pores. Parallel systems will use the same polymers physisorbed on non-porous metal oxides (e.g., ZnO) or free in solution. Local pH changes will be probed in situ using a combination of high-temperature water vapor sorption analysis with integrated pH-sensitive fluorescent probes (e.g., fluorescein derivatives) and operando infrared spectroscopy to monitor protonation states under dynamic gas flow (e.g., C3H6/CO2 mixtures).
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a definitive, mechanistic link between nanoconfinement in porous materials and the efficacy of polymer-induced local acid/base phenomena. It will provide design principles for creating 'pH switches' in ultramicroporous materials for advanced separation and sensing, directly informing the development of more efficient chemiresistive sensors and purification systems.
proposals.novelty: While Kitagawa's group has pioneered the study of water sorption and hydrocarbon recognition in soft porous crystals, directly isolating and proving the necessity of confinement for polymer-mediated pH effects—by comparing identical polymers in confined vs. unconfined states—is a novel and critical experimental test.

proposals.title: Programmable Microenvironmental Acidity in Soft Porous Crystals via Polymer@MOF Composites for Enhanced Catalytic Hydrolysis
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026, Kitagawa's work on soft porous crystals (SPCs) and hybrid ultramicroporous materials has demonstrated exceptional responsiveness to guest molecules like water. However, precise, dynamic control over the chemical microenvironment (e.g., local pH) within these pores for catalysis remains a challenge. Tailoring this microenvironment is crucial for advancing applications in chemical separations and sensing, as suggested by his work on hydrocarbon recognition and water sorption.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the molecular weight (chain length) and loading density of poly(acrylic acid) confined within a flexible, water-responsive MOF (e.g., ELM-11) will dictate the programmable local acidity, which can be optimized to dramatically enhance the catalytic rate of ester hydrolysis reactions under humid conditions.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a series of composites by infiltrating poly(acrylic acid) of varying molecular weights (1k-100k Da) at controlled loadings into the prototypal diamondoid topology material [Cu(4,4'-bipyridine)₂(OTf)₂]. Using dynamic vapor sorption (DVS), we will correlate water uptake with framework flexibility and polymer swelling. The local pH will be probed using confocal fluorescence microscopy with pH-sensitive dyes (e.g., fluorescein). Catalytic activity will be evaluated for the hydrolysis of 4-nitrophenyl acetate, monitored via UV-Vis spectroscopy, under controlled temperature and humidity.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a design principle for creating 'smart' catalytic pores with tunable, stimulus-responsive acidity. It will directly link polymer physicochemistry to quantifiable microenvironmental properties and reaction kinetics in MOFs, advancing the field of composite porous materials for on-demand catalysis. The findings will inform the development of next-generation chemiresistive sensors where local pH changes modulate conductivity.
proposals.novelty: While polymer-MOF composites exist, systematically using polymer chain length and confinement to *program* the dynamic pH of a flexible, water-responsive MOF for catalysis is unexplored. This moves beyond static functionalization to create an adaptive, synergistic system where both host (MOF) and guest (polymer) cooperatively respond to humidity.

proposals.title: Confinement-Enhanced Nanozyme Activity: Extending the Polyacid/Polybase Strategy to Metal Oxide and Carbon-Based Systems
proposals.motivation: Nanozymes, nanomaterials with enzyme-like catalytic activity, hold immense promise for biosensing and environmental remediation, but their activity and substrate selectivity often lag behind natural enzymes. Pre-2026 work, including Kitagawa's on soft porous crystals and water sorption, demonstrates that precise chemical confinement within porous frameworks can dramatically alter molecular recognition and reactivity. This proposal seeks to apply this powerful confinement principle, proven in MOFs, to enhance the performance of more robust but less tunable nanozyme classes.
proposals.hypothesis: That the deliberate creation of a polyacidic or polybasic chemical environment within the pores of non-MOF nanozymes (e.g., metal oxides, carbon nanotubes) will significantly enhance their intrinsic peroxidase- or oxidase-like activity by pre-organizing and activating substrate molecules, akin to the active site of natural enzymes.
proposals.method: We will synthesize and characterize metal oxide (e.g., CeO2, Fe3O4) and nitrogen-doped carbon nanotube nanozymes. Using pre-2026 surface functionalization techniques, we will graft polyelectrolyte brushes (e.g., poly(acrylic acid) as polyacid, poly(allylamine) as polybase) onto their surfaces and within their mesopores. The catalytic activity will be rigorously tested using standard chromogenic peroxidase (TMB/H2O2) and oxidase (TMB/O2) assays. We will correlate activity with sorption isotherms of water and organic substrates under dynamic conditions, employing in-situ spectroscopic techniques (FTIR, Raman) to probe substrate activation and binding energetics within the functionalized pores.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a generalizable design strategy for engineering high-activity nanozymes beyond the traditional focus on metal-centers or doping alone. It will provide fundamental insights into the role of localized chemical environment and substrate pre-concentration in nanomaterial catalysis, bridging the fields of porous materials science and nanozyme research.
proposals.novelty: While confinement effects are well-studied in MOFs, their systematic application to modulate the catalytic mechanism of inorganic and carbon-based nanozymes via surface-grafted polyelectrolyte environments is a novel and unexplored approach, moving beyond intrinsic material composition to extrinsic chemical programming.

proposals.title: Core-Sheath Inversion in Hybrid Composites: MOF-Core/Metal-Oxide-Sheath Architectures for Enhanced Water Vapor Sorption and Chemiresistive Sensing
proposals.motivation: Hybrid materials combining metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) and metal oxides (MOs) are promising for sensing and sorption, but conventional architectures feature an MO core with a MOF sheath. This geometry may limit performance by restricting analyte access to the MOF's pores or creating inefficient interfacial charge transfer pathways. Inverting this structure could fundamentally alter diffusion kinetics and electronic interactions, offering a new route to optimize material properties for applications like humidity sensing or hydrocarbon purification.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that constructing a composite with a porous MOF core and a continuous metal oxide sheath will enhance water vapor sorption kinetics and selectivity, while also improving the stability and electrical transduction efficiency of the composite for chemiresistive sensing compared to traditional MO-core structures.
proposals.method: We will synthesize the inverted composites using a stepwise approach: first, preparing uniform microcrystals of prototypal diamondoid topology hybrid ultramicroporous materials (e.g., SIFSIX-3-Ni) or soft porous crystals (e.g., ELM-11) as the core. Subsequently, a conformal sheath of a semiconducting metal oxide (e.g., SnO2 or ZnO) will be deposited via atomic layer deposition (ALD) or sol-gel methods at elevated temperatures to ensure a continuous, thin coating. The materials will be characterized by PXRD, SEM/TEM, and N2/water vapor sorption. Performance will be evaluated through dynamic water vapor sorption cycles and by fabricating chemiresistive sensors to test their response to humidity and target hydrocarbons (e.g., C3H6).
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new synthetic paradigm for MOF-metal oxide composites, providing fundamental insights into how core-sheath geometry governs mass transport and electronic communication. It will deliver a novel material platform with potentially superior sorption kinetics and sensor stability, advancing the design of next-generation porous and electronic materials for environmental monitoring.
proposals.novelty: While core-sheath composites are known, using the MOF as the structural core and the metal oxide as the functional sheath is a deliberate inversion of the established design logic, creating a distinct class of materials where the porous framework is fully encapsulated yet electronically interfaced, which is non-obvious from prior work focused on MOF coatings.

proposals.title: Programmable Pore Chemistry in Soft Porous Coordination Polymers for the Selective Pre-concentration of Non-Explosive Volatile Organic Compounds
proposals.motivation: The detection of trace volatile organic compounds (VOCs) for medical diagnostics, environmental monitoring, and food safety requires efficient pre-concentration. While MOFs are excellent adsorbents, their rigid pores often lack the adaptive selectivity needed for complex VOC mixtures. Kitagawa's work on soft porous crystals (SPCs) and dynamic frameworks, which respond to guest molecules, offers a unique platform to address this challenge.
proposals.hypothesis: By systematically engineering the pore chemistry and flexibility of MOF-based sheaths using nitro-group programming and diamondoid topology design, we can achieve inverse hydrocarbon recognition and selectively pre-concentrate specific non-explosive VOCs (e.g., aldehydes, ketones, aromatics) from humid air.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a series of hybrid ultramicroporous materials based on pillared-layer diamondoid topologies, incorporating functional organic linkers with nitro, amino, and carboxyl groups to modulate host-guest interactions. High-temperature activation protocols will be employed to ensure clean pores. Dynamic water and VOC sorption isotherms will be measured to map framework flexibility and selectivity. The binding energy informatics approach, correlating density of states from DFT calculations with experimental sorption data, will guide the optimization of pore chemistry for target VOCs like acetone, toluene, and hexanal.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a design principle for creating adaptive MOF sheaths tailored for pre-concentrating specific VOC classes, moving beyond explosive detection. It will provide a fundamental understanding of how programmed flexibility and pore chemistry govern selective recognition in humid environments, advancing materials for next-generation chemiresistive sensor arrays.
proposals.novelty: The approach uniquely combines the concepts of soft porosity and inverse hydrocarbon recognition, previously explored for light hydrocarbons, for the selective capture of polar and non-polar oxygenated VOCs under realistic humid conditions, a significant extension of Kitagawa's recent work.

proposals.title: Generalizing the Self-Promoting Sensing Phenomenon: A Systematic Investigation of Light-Activated MOF-Metal Oxide Chemiresistive Composites
proposals.motivation: Recent work by Kitagawa's group and others has demonstrated that certain metal-organic framework (MOF)-metal oxide chemiresistive sensors exhibit a unique 'self-promoting' behavior under light irradiation, where sensing performance improves over successive cycles. This phenomenon, potentially linked to dynamic structural or electronic changes, could revolutionize the design of adaptive, high-performance sensors. However, it remains unknown whether this mechanism is specific to a single material system or represents a general principle applicable to other porous, light-responsive composites, limiting its broader exploitation.
proposals.hypothesis: The self-promoting sensing mechanism is a generalizable phenomenon that occurs in a wide range of light-activated MOF-metal oxide composites, driven by photo-induced modifications to the interfacial density of states and binding energy landscapes that enhance analyte recognition and charge transfer.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a series of MOF-metal oxide composites, selecting MOFs with known photoactive units (e.g., based on Zr, Cu, or Fe) and varied pore chemistry (e.g., nitro-functionalized for inverse recognition) combined with standard n-type and p-type metal oxides (e.g., ZnO, SnO2, NiO). These will be fabricated into chemiresistive devices. Using a custom high-throughput testing system, we will perform dynamic sorption and resistance measurements under controlled humidity and temperature, with synchronized UV-Vis light activation. The key is to apply the Density of States and Binding Energy Informatics framework from recent work to analyze the electronic structure changes before and after cyclic light-gas exposure, correlating these shifts with the evolution of sensitivity, selectivity, and response time.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish the scope and fundamental principles of the self-promoting effect in hybrid porous materials, moving it from a curious observation to a design strategy. It will produce a curated library of composites validated for adaptive sensing and identify the critical material descriptors (e.g., band alignment, soft porosity, defect density) that govern the phenomenon.
proposals.novelty: While self-promotion has been noted anecdotally, this is the first systematic study to test its generality across diverse MOF topologies and oxides, explicitly linking it to pre-2026 informatics methods for electronic structure analysis to uncover a unified mechanism.

proposals.title: Programmable Flexibility: Tuning Water Vapor Sorption Hysteresis and Dynamics in Interpenetrated MOFs via Extended, Aliphatic Dicarboxylate Linkers
proposals.motivation: The development of porous materials for efficient atmospheric water harvesting and humidity control requires precise control over water sorption isotherms, particularly the pressure and width of hysteresis loops. While Kitagawa's work on ultramicroporous materials has established structure-property relationships for rigid frameworks, the role of framework flexibility and interpenetration in modulating sorption dynamics, especially for water, remains less explored. Materials with tunable hysteretic behavior are critical for next-generation adsorption-driven heat pumps and dehumidification systems.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that employing longer, flexible aliphatic dicarboxylate linkers (e.g., adipate, suberate, azelate) with appropriate metal clusters will direct the formation of interpenetrated metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) whose pore volume and dynamic flexibility can be systematically tuned, leading to predictable and programmable water vapor sorption hysteresis and uptake kinetics.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a series of MOFs using Zn(II) or Cu(II) paddlewheel clusters and the aforementioned linear dicarboxylates under solvothermal conditions. The synthesis will be guided by high-throughput screening of reaction parameters (temperature, solvent, modulator) to target interpenetrated structures. Materials will be characterized by SCXRD, PXRD, and N2/Ar physisorption. Water vapor sorption isotherms will be measured at multiple temperatures (25-65°C) to analyze hysteresis and calculate isosteric heats of adsorption. The dynamics of water uptake/release will be quantified using dynamic vapor sorption (DVS) analysis, correlating linker length and flexibility with kinetic profiles.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new design principle for soft porous crystals (SPCs) based on linker flexibility and interpenetration, specifically for water sorption. It will deliver a family of materials with engineered hysteresis, contributing directly to the informatics of binding energy and dynamics for vapor sensing and capture. The findings will expand the toolkit for designing responsive porous materials beyond rigid, aromatic linkers.
proposals.novelty: While interpenetration is often avoided to maximize pore volume, this proposal deliberately exploits it as a design element, combining it with the under-explored use of long, flexible aliphatic linkers—a departure from Kitagawa's recent work on aromatic or nitro-functionalized linkers—to create MOFs with unique, predictable water sorption dynamics.

proposals.title: Inverse Design of Hierarchically Porous Carbon and Metal Oxide Replicas via Programmable Soft Porous Crystal Sacrificial Templates
proposals.motivation: The synthesis of porous carbons and metal oxides with precise, hierarchical pore architectures remains a significant challenge, often requiring complex templating methods.
