Soft Porous Crystals (SPCs), particularly flexible metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) like those pioneered by Kitagawa, exhibit dynamic, stimuli-responsive porosity but are typically studied for their intrinsic sorption properties. Leveraging their well-defined, tunable structures as sacrificial templates offers a novel route to create replicas with inherited, complex porosity for applications in energy storage and gas separation where traditional templates fail.
proposals.hypothesis: That the dynamic structural transformations and organic/inorganic composition of specific SPCs can be exploited as sacrificial templates to create porous carbon or metal oxide replicas, where the template's removal process (e.g., thermal decomposition) directly translates its unique pore geometry and chemical functionality into the replica material.
proposals.method: We will select prototypal diamondoid topology hybrid ultramicroporous materials and nitro group-programmed SPCs as sacrificial templates. These will be infiltrated with carbon precursors (e.g., furfuryl alcohol) or metal salt solutions. Controlled pyrolysis or calcination under inert or oxidative atmospheres will be performed, with in-situ PXRD and thermogravimetric analysis to monitor the dynamic template removal and replica formation. The resulting replicas will be characterized by N2/H2O sorption, electron microscopy, and XPS to correlate template structure with replica porosity and surface chemistry.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new 'inverse design' paradigm for porous material synthesis, moving beyond using MOFs as final materials to using them as programmable, sacrificial templates. It will deliver a family of hierarchically porous carbons and metal oxides with unprecedented control over micro- and mesoporosity derived from the template's dynamic behavior, directly impacting the design of next-generation chemiresistive sensor supports and selective adsorbents.
proposals.novelty: While MOFs have been used as hard templates, using *soft*, dynamically responsive porous crystals as sacrificial templates is unexplored. The novelty lies in programming the replica's pore architecture via the template's stimulus-induced structural changes (gate-opening, breathing) and its organic linker-derived chemical functionality, which is not possible with conventional silica or rigid MOF templates.

proposals.title: Computational Prediction of Non-Porous to Porous Phase Transitions in Soft Coordination Polymers for Adaptive Water Sorption
proposals.motivation: Soft porous crystals (SPCs), such as Kitagawa's prototypal diamondoid topology materials, exhibit dynamic structural transitions upon guest sorption, crucial for applications like humidity control. However, the fundamental understanding of the transition pathways and the stability of the non-porous phases remains limited, hindering the rational design of materials with tailored sorption steps and hysteresis. Predicting these phases and their interconversion energetics computationally would provide a powerful design tool for next-generation adaptive porous materials.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that crystal structure prediction (CSP) methods can reliably identify metastable non-porous polymorphs of known flexible metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) and map the minimum energy pathways for their transformation to porous phases, with the transition barriers correlating with observed sorption hysteresis.
proposals.method: We will employ state-of-the-art CSP using the FHI-AIMS code for DFT-level accuracy and the AIRSS package for initial structure generation. For selected SPCs (e.g., based on the prototypal diamondoid topology), we will perform CSP in the absence of guest molecules to predict stable and metastable non-porous polymorphs. Subsequently, we will use the nudged elastic band (NEB) method to calculate the transition pathways and energy barriers between the predicted non-porous phases and their known porous counterparts. Molecular dynamics simulations at high temperature will be used to validate the dynamic stability of predicted phases.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver the first computational atlas of non-porous polymorphs for key flexible MOF systems, directly linking their predicted stability to experimental sorption properties. It will establish a methodology to *a priori* design or select materials with desired gate-opening pressures and hysteresis for water vapor sorption, moving beyond trial-and-error synthesis. The predicted transition pathways will offer unprecedented atomic-level insight into the mechanism of flexibility.
proposals.novelty: While CSP is commonly used for predicting stable porous frameworks, its targeted application to discover and characterize the *non-porous* phases of flexible MOFs and their transition pathways is novel. This inverts the typical design paradigm, using computational instability to understand and engineer dynamic function.

proposals.title: Thermally Enhanced Molecular Sieving: Exploiting Framework Dynamics for High-Temperature Selective Adsorption in Soft Porous Crystals
proposals.motivation: Adsorptive gas separation, crucial for industrial purification and carbon capture, is fundamentally limited by the exothermic nature of physisorption, where selectivity typically decreases with rising temperature. This necessitates energy-intensive cooling, increasing operational costs and complexity. Overcoming this thermodynamic constraint would revolutionize process design, enabling efficient separations in high-temperature industrial streams or under dynamic thermal conditions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that in flexible porous coordination polymers (PCPs) or soft porous crystals, a temperature-induced structural transition can be programmed to selectively gate or enhance the adsorption of a target molecule over its competitor, thereby inverting the typical negative correlation between temperature and separation factor.
proposals.method: We will design and synthesize a series of nitro-functionalized, diamondoid-topology hybrid ultramicroporous materials (HUMs), inspired by Kitagawa's recent work on inverse hydrocarbon recognition. High-temperature, dynamic water vapor sorption analysis will probe framework flexibility. The separation performance for challenging pairs like C3H6/C3H8 or CO2/CH4 will be evaluated using variable-temperature breakthrough experiments coupled with in-situ X-ray diffraction and calorimetry. This will correlate thermal energy input with specific host-guest interactions and gate-opening pressures to map a 'selectivity-temperature phase diagram'.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new design principle for 'thermo-responsive adsorbents' where temperature acts as a positive operational switch. It will provide a fundamental understanding of how to harness entropy and framework dynamics for separation. The outcome is a material platform for more energy- and operationally-efficient adsorption processes.
proposals.novelty: This directly challenges the canonical van't Hoff relation in adsorption by seeking materials where the separation factor (α) increases with T, moving beyond optimizing isosteric heat to actively exploiting thermal energy to drive selective structural changes.

proposals.title: Thermally Responsive Gate-Opening in MOFs via Polymer-Inspired Organic Linker Design
proposals.motivation: Stimuli-responsive porous materials, particularly those responding to temperature, are crucial for advanced gas storage, separation, and sensing applications. While soft porous crystals (SPCs) exhibit dynamic structural changes, their response is often binary and triggered by guest adsorption, not temperature itself. Translating the well-established, tunable thermal response of polymers like poly(N-isopropylacrylamide) (PNIPAM) into the crystalline, porous domain of metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) remains an unexplored frontier for creating precisely controlled, temperature-gated pores.
proposals.hypothesis: By incorporating organic linkers with oligo(ethylene glycol) or short alkylamide side chains that undergo a conformational or hydration-state change at a specific temperature, we can design MOFs and hybrid ultramicroporous materials that exhibit a sharp, reversible pore-opening transition in response to temperature alone, independent of guest loading.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a series of ditopic organic linkers functionalized with thermoresponsive side chains, such as diethylglycolamides or oligo(ethylene oxide) units. These will be reacted with metal ions (e.g., Zn²⁺, Cu²⁺) and/or inorganic pillars (e.g., [Fe(CN)₆]⁴⁻) to form pillared-layer or diamondoid-topology frameworks. The dynamic sorption properties will be characterized using high-resolution, temperature-controlled water and hydrocarbon vapor isotherms. In-situ variable-temperature PXRD and solid-state NMR will be used to correlate the macroscopic sorption step with microscopic linker motion and framework flexibility.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new design principle for creating intrinsically thermoresponsive porous crystals, moving beyond guest-induced flexibility. It will deliver a novel class of materials with temperature-tunable pore apertures for applications in smart membranes and thermal swing separations. The project will also bridge the conceptual gap between the responsive dynamics of amorphous polymers and crystalline porous materials.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the direct translation of polymer thermoresponse mechanisms—conformational change and dehydration of side chains—into the rigid, ordered backbone of a crystalline porous framework, creating a thermal stimulus as the primary trigger for pore gating, rather than a secondary effect of adsorption.

proposals.title: Propylene-Gated Soft Porous Crystals: An Inverse Design for Dynamic Porosity Control in Hydrocarbon Separations
proposals.motivation: The purification of light hydrocarbons like propylene (C3H6) is a critical, energy-intensive industrial process. While Kitagawa's recent work demonstrates the use of nitro-functionalized soft porous crystals (SPCs) for one-step C3H6 purification via inverse hydrocarbon recognition, the potential of using the adsorbed C3H6 itself as a structural trigger remains unexplored. This inverse problem—using the target adsorbate to dynamically reconfigure the host framework—could unlock new adaptive separation mechanisms with on-demand selectivity.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the selective adsorption of propylene into a nitro-functionalized, flexible metal-organic framework (MOF) will induce a specific, cooperative structural transition (gate-opening) that systematically modulates the effective pore size and chemistry, thereby tuning the material's subsequent adsorption affinity for other small molecules, such as propane (C3H4) or water vapor.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a series of nitro-decorated, diamondoid topology hybrid ultramicroporous materials (HUMs) analogous to those reported by Kitagawa, known for their water and hydrocarbon sorption dynamics. High-resolution in situ synchrotron X-ray diffraction and Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy will be employed under controlled C3H6 partial pressures and elevated temperatures to map the structural phase diagram and binding site evolution. Dynamic vapor sorption (DVS) analysis will then quantify the subsequent, propylene-induced changes in the sorption isotherms and kinetics for probe molecules (C3H4, H2O, N2), directly linking framework dynamics to tunable separation performance.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel 'adsorbate-as-trigger' paradigm for post-synthetic modulation of porosity in soft crystals, moving beyond static design. It will provide a fundamental understanding of how a primary guest molecule can be used to program a material's secondary adsorption properties, contributing directly to the development of intelligent, multi-stage separation systems and responsive sensors.
proposals.novelty: The approach is novel in its explicit inversion of the stimulus-response sequence in flexible MOFs; instead of using an external stimulus (e.g., pressure, temperature) to control adsorption of a target gas, we use the selective adsorption of the target gas itself (C3H6) as the internal stimulus to control the material's properties for other gases.

proposals.title: High-Throughput Computational Discovery of Bimetallic MOF Composites for Tunable Electromagnetic Wave Absorption
proposals.motivation: The proliferation of electronic devices and wireless technologies has created a critical need for advanced electromagnetic (EM) wave absorbing materials to mitigate interference and pollution. Metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) offer exceptional structural and compositional tunability, but the vast design space for bimetallic MOF composites for EM absorption remains largely unexplored, making experimental trial-and-error inefficient.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that specific combinations of bimetallic nodes and organic linkers in MOFs can be computationally identified to optimize dielectric and magnetic losses, leading to MOF composites with superior and tunable EM absorption properties across the GHz frequency range.
proposals.method: We will employ a high-throughput screening workflow, inspired by heterogeneous catalysis studies. First, DFT (VASP, using GGA-PBE) will calculate key electronic structure descriptors (e.g., band gap, density of states, polarizability) for a curated library of bimetallic MOF nodes (e.g., M1M2(BDC)2, M = Zn, Cu, Ni, Co, Fe). These descriptors, along with topological and chemical features, will train a graph neural network (GNN) model to predict complex permittivity/permeability. The model will be validated against limited experimental data from the literature and used to screen thousands of virtual bimetallic MOF compositions, ranking them by predicted absorption performance.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first dedicated computational pipeline for rational design of MOF-based EM absorbers, identifying promising, non-intuitive bimetallic compositions for synthesis. It will establish structure-property relationships linking metal node chemistry to EM loss mechanisms, accelerating the development of lightweight, high-performance, and frequency-tunable absorbing materials.
proposals.novelty: While high-throughput screening is established for gas sorption and catalysis in MOFs, its application to EM property prediction is novel. The focus on bimetallic nodes for fine-tuning both dielectric and magnetic properties within a single porous framework represents a significant advance over trial-and-error studies of monometallic or blended composites.

proposals.title: Postsynthetic Alkaline Transformation of Flexible PCPs for Engineering Dual-Function Water/Hydrocarbon Sorption Channels
proposals.motivation: Porous coordination polymers (PCPs) with dynamic, stimuli-responsive frameworks offer unique potential for selective gas sorption and separation, particularly for water vapor and light hydrocarbons like C3H6. However, rationally engineering specific, multi-functional pore environments within these materials remains a significant synthetic challenge. A recently reported method using boiling alkaline solution to induce a crystal transformation in a specific PCP created a material with dual-function channels, suggesting a potentially generalizable route to pore engineering.
proposals.hypothesis: The postsynthetic alkaline transformation method is not limited to its prototypal system and can be applied to other flexible PCPs with labile metal-linker bonds, predictably generating new topologies and pore chemistries that enhance or modulate water and hydrocarbon sorption properties.
proposals.method: We will select a series of flexible PCPs based on Zn(II) or Cu(II) with carboxylate and/or pyridyl-based linkers, known for their dynamic sorption behavior. These will be subjected to the established protocol of treatment in boiling aqueous KOH/NaOH solutions under varied concentrations and durations. The transformed materials will be characterized by SCXRD, PXRD, and gas/vapor sorption analysis (H2O, N2, C3H6, C3H8) at relevant temperatures. The sorption data will be analyzed using established models (e.g., Dubinin-Astakhov, IAST) to quantify uptake, selectivity, and binding energies.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish the scope and limitations of alkaline postsynthetic transformation as a tool for PCP engineering. It will yield new materials with tailored pore geometries and chemical environments, directly contributing to the design of advanced sorbents for atmospheric water harvesting or energy-efficient hydrocarbon purification, as evidenced by Kitagawa's recent work on water sorption and C3H6 purification.
proposals.novelty: While postsynthetic modification is common, using harsh alkaline conditions to induce a complete, single-crystal-to-single-crystal topological transformation in flexible PCPs is a nascent and underexplored strategy. Extending this method to other PCP families to systematically create dual-function channels is a novel and non-obvious application of this dynamic process.

proposals.title: Elucidating the Cooperative Trap-and-Flow Mechanism in Soft Porous Crystals via Systematic Molecular Probe Analysis
proposals.motivation: Kitagawa's recent work on soft porous crystals (SPCs) like the nitro-functionalized framework demonstrated a novel 'cooperative trap-and-flow' mechanism for separating C2H2/CO2 mixtures, where one gas is trapped to open pores for the other. This mechanism is crucial for energy-efficient separations but is poorly understood beyond the specific C2H2/CO2 pair. A systematic investigation using diverse probe molecules is needed to establish design principles for next-generation adaptive adsorbents.
proposals.hypothesis: The efficiency and selectivity of the cooperative trap-and-flow mechanism in flexible metal-organic frameworks are governed by a critical interplay between the size, shape, and chemical functionality of the probe molecules, which dictates the framework's dynamic structural response and guest-guest cooperativity.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a series of nitro-functionalized soft porous crystals analogous to the reported system. High-pressure, variable-temperature sorption isotherms will be measured for probe molecules of varying size (C1-C4), shape (linear, branched, cyclic), and polarity (e.g., C2H4, C3H6, C3H8, SO2, NO). In-situ synchrotron X-ray diffraction and Raman spectroscopy will be employed under dynamic sorption conditions to correlate guest uptake with framework deformation. Grand Canonical Monte Carlo (GCMC) simulations with flexible force fields will model the cooperative adsorption process.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a comprehensive molecular-level understanding of the trap-and-flow mechanism, establishing a 'guest-shape/size-property' relationship for dynamic SPCs. It will deliver design rules for tailoring SPCs for challenging one-step purifications of industrially relevant gas pairs beyond acetylene/carbon dioxide, advancing the field of adaptive separation materials.
proposals.novelty: While the trap-and-flow concept is known for one system, a systematic study varying molecular probe geometry to decode the mechanism's fundamental rules is unprecedented. It moves from observing a phenomenon to establishing a predictive framework for its engineering.

proposals.title: Exploiting Programmable Ligand Lability for Dynamic Water Sorption and Hydrocarbon Separation in Soft Porous Crystals
proposals.motivation: The instability of organic ligands in metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) is typically viewed as a limitation for long-term applications. However, pre-2026 work by Kitagawa and others on soft porous crystals (SPCs) demonstrates that structural flexibility enables unique gas sorption properties. This proposal re-conceptualizes ligand instability as a design feature to create frameworks where controlled, stimuli-responsive bond cleavage or rearrangement triggers dramatic pore-structure transformations, unlocking new dynamic sorption behaviors not possible with rigid, stable MOFs.
proposals.hypothesis: By intentionally incorporating organic ligands with thermally or chemically labile bonds (e.g., certain imine, ester, or azo linkages) into metal-organic or hybrid ultramicroporous frameworks, we can synthesize materials whose porosity and sorption profiles undergo irreversible, stepwise changes in response to specific environmental triggers, such as temperature or hydrocarbon exposure, enabling novel separation and sensing functions.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a series of Zn(II) or Cu(II) frameworks using ditopic ligands with systematically varied labile functional groups (e.g., thermally cleavable tert-butyl esters, hydrolytically sensitive imines). High-temperature in situ powder X-ray diffraction and dynamic water vapor sorption analysis will monitor structural transitions and binding site evolution. The 'inverse hydrocarbon recognition' concept will be extended by using nitro-group-programmed ligands whose lability is selectively enhanced upon binding specific olefins (e.g., propylene), tested via breakthrough column experiments for C3H6/C3H8 purification.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new design paradigm for 'transient porosity' materials, where engineered instability creates advanced functions. It will deliver prototype sorbents with tunable, irreversible sorption steps for energy-efficient drying or hydrocarbon separation. Furthermore, it will provide fundamental insights into the relationship between ligand degradation pathways and collective framework dynamics.
proposals.novelty: While prior work on SPCs focuses on reversible flexibility, this proposal deliberately designs irreversible, ligand-driven structural metamorphosis as a functional mechanism. It uniquely combines the concepts of ultramicroporosity, inverse recognition, and programmed ligand lability into a single material strategy for stimuli-responsive sorption.

proposals.title: A Design Informatics Platform for High-Temperature Water Vapor Sorption in Programmable Soft Porous Crystals
proposals.motivation: Efficient small molecule adsorption, particularly of water vapor at elevated temperatures, is critical for industrial heat management and atmospheric water harvesting. While hybrid frameworks like Metal-Organic Frameworks (MOFs) and Soft Porous Crystals (SPCs) offer tunability, a systematic design framework to predict and optimize adsorption for specific, challenging conditions remains underdeveloped. This gap limits the rapid discovery of materials for targeted applications such as high-temperature dehumidification or one-step purification of industrial gas streams.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that integrating density of states (DOS) informatics with dynamic sorption analysis will reveal quantifiable structure-property relationships, enabling the inverse design of hybrid frameworks with programmed functional sites for optimal, selective small molecule adsorption under non-ambient conditions.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a library of nitro-group-functionalized, diamondoid-topology hybrid ultramicroporous materials, building on Kitagawa's recent work. High-temperature water vapor sorption isotherms will be measured dynamically to assess stability and uptake. Concurrently, we will perform density functional theory (DFT) calculations to compute the electronic density of states (DOS) and binding energies for water and target hydrocarbons at framework binding sites. A machine learning model will be trained on this combined dataset to correlate structural/electronic descriptors (e.g., linker functionality, metal node, pore geometry, DOS features) with high-temperature sorption performance, creating a predictive design algorithm.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel informatics-driven design framework that moves beyond trial-and-error in porous material discovery. It will deliver new fundamental insights into the role of electronic structure in high-temperature guest-framework interactions and produce experimentally validated, programmable soft porous crystals with record performance for water vapor sorption under industrially relevant conditions.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the explicit fusion of electronic structure (DOS) informatics with dynamic sorption data at high temperature to guide the design of SPCs, a shift from purely geometric or chemical intuition to a computationally guided, multi-property optimization strategy for adsorption.

proposals.title: Geometric Control of Water Sorption Hysteresis in Soft Porous Coordination Polymers via Targeted Metal Coordination Sphere Engineering
proposals.motivation: The dynamic, stimuli-responsive nature of soft porous crystals (SPCs) is crucial for advanced vapor sorption and separation, but precise control over their switching pressure and hysteresis remains a fundamental challenge. Pre-2026 work, including Kitagawa's on ultramicroporous materials and inverse hydrocarbon recognition, highlights the role of host-guest interactions, yet a systematic study linking specific metal coordination geometries to framework flexibility and water sorption properties is lacking.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the geometry and lability of the metal coordination sphere (e.g., octahedral vs. square pyramidal vs. tetrahedral) can be deliberately engineered to dictate the energy barrier for framework transformation, thereby allowing predictable tuning of water sorption isotherm steps and hysteresis width.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a series of isoreticular 2D or 3D frameworks using a single, ditopic organic linker (e.g., a functionalized bipyridine) paired with different metal ions (e.g., Zn²⁺, Cu²⁺, Ni²⁺, Co²⁺) under solvothermal conditions to induce varying coordination geometries. High-temperature in situ variable-temperature PXRD and single-crystal X-ray diffraction will be used to characterize the dynamic structural changes. Water vapor sorption isotherms will be measured to correlate hysteresis loops with the specific metal coordination environment, supported by density functional theory (DFT) calculations on the metal-linker binding energy and framework deformation pathways.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a design principle for 'geometry-programmed' soft porous materials, moving beyond linker functionalization to direct metal-node control. It will provide a predictive map linking coordination chemistry to sorption performance, advancing the development of next-generation humidity buffers and selective adsorbents with tailored switching behaviors.
proposals.novelty: While metal substitution is common, this proposal uniquely focuses on exploiting the inherent geometric lability of specific metal coordination spheres as the primary variable to program framework dynamics and sorption hysteresis, a targeted approach distinct from empirical linker modification.

proposals.title: Inverse Design of Flexible Organic Ligands for Target Water Vapor Sorption Isotherms in Hybrid Ultramicroporous Materials
proposals.motivation: The rational design of porous coordination polymers (PCPs) or MOFs for specific gas sorption profiles, especially for water vapor, remains a major challenge. Current approaches rely on trial-and-error synthesis of candidate ligands, which is inefficient for discovering materials with optimal dynamic adsorption properties, such as steep uptake steps at desired relative humidities for atmospheric water harvesting or dehumidification.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by framing ligand design as an inverse problem, a computational workflow can reverse-engineer optimal organic linker structures that, when coordinated to specific metal ions, will yield soft porous crystals with a pre-defined, target water vapor sorption isotherm.
proposals.method: We will develop a generative deep learning model, specifically a conditional variational autoencoder (CVAE), trained on a large database of known MOF/PCP structures and their simulated water sorption isotherms (using Grand Canonical Monte Carlo with validated force fields). The model's latent space will encode the relationship between ligand topology/functional groups and key sorption profile features (e.g., gate-opening pressure, step height). The inverse design will be achieved by conditioning the CVAE decoder on a target isotherm to generate novel, synthetically plausible ligand candidates, which will then be validated via molecular simulation and prioritized for synthesis.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel computational paradigm for the targeted discovery of dynamic sorbents, moving beyond structure prediction to direct function-led design. It will provide a concrete set of design rules and candidate ligands for humidity-control materials, significantly accelerating the development cycle for next-generation porous materials.
proposals.novelty: While high-throughput screening of existing MOF databases is common, the active inverse generation of entirely new organic ligands from a target dynamic sorption profile—particularly for water—is a fundamentally unexplored and non-obvious approach that directly addresses the core challenge of materials discovery.

proposals.title: Adsorption-Induced Structural Dynamics in Soft Porous Crystals: Probing Framework Flexibility via Water and Hydrocarbon Sorption Isotherms
proposals.motivation: The dynamic structural response of flexible porous frameworks, such as soft porous crystals (SPCs), to guest adsorption is central to their function in separation and sensing. However, the detailed mechanistic link between specific adsorption events (e.g., pore filling, gate-opening) and the real-time dynamics of the host lattice remains poorly understood. A method to use adsorption itself as an in-situ probe would provide unprecedented insight into these stimuli-responsive materials.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the detailed shape and hysteresis of high-resolution water and light hydrocarbon (C2-C3) sorption isotherms, measured across a range of temperatures, encode specific signatures of framework dynamic modes, including hinge motion, ligand rotation, and layer sliding.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a series of isoreticular, diamondoid-topology hybrid ultramicroporous materials with tuned organic linkers to systematically vary flexibility. High-precision, variable-temperature (253–373 K) water and hydrocarbon vapor sorption isotherms will be collected. The data will be analyzed using a novel informatics approach, correlating isotherm inflection points, step positions, and hysteresis loop shapes with framework dynamics predicted by molecular dynamics simulations and in-situ X-ray diffraction measurements during adsorption.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new analytical paradigm where sorption isotherms are used as a primary diagnostic tool for framework flexibility, moving beyond pure capacity metrics. It will yield design principles for tailoring dynamic response in SPCs for advanced separations, such as the one-step purification of C3H6, and for creating more responsive chemiresistive sensor materials.
proposals.novelty: While adsorption is typically used to characterize static pore properties, this proposal treats the adsorption process itself as a dynamic mechanical probe of the host framework, a concept not fully explored in pre-2026 literature. The combination of targeted ultramicroporous materials, precise sorption of challenging guests like water, and dynamic informatics analysis is uniquely positioned to decode adsorption-induced flexibility.

proposals.title: Stimuli-Responsive, Amorphous Polymer Gels and Porous Liquids for Switchable Water Vapor Diffusion and Sorption
proposals.motivation: Crystalline porous frameworks like MOFs dominate research into switchable sorbents, but their synthesis and stability, especially under humid conditions, present challenges. Amorphous polymers and the emerging concept of porous liquids offer potential for more processable and robust materials, yet their ability to exhibit sharp, stimuli-responsive diffusion regulation akin to soft porous crystals remains largely unexplored. This work addresses the critical need for tunable water vapor capture and release in applications like atmospheric water harvesting and humidity control.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by incorporating dynamic, stimuli-responsive functional groups (e.g., nitro groups, carboxylates) into amorphous polymer networks or porous liquid matrices, we can achieve a reversible, order-disorder transition at the molecular scale that gates the diffusion of water vapor, mimicking the function of crystalline frameworks without requiring long-range order.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a library of amorphous polyimide and polyamide gels with integrated thermo- or photo-responsive moieties, characterizing porosity via high-pressure sorption and PALS. Parallelly, we will design Type III porous liquids using surface-functionalized porous nanoparticles (e.g., amorphous silica) dispersed in a sterically hindered ionic liquid. Water vapor sorption isotherms will be measured dynamically across a temperature range (25-80°C) to assess switchability. The diffusion kinetics will be modeled using a modified Maxwell-Stefan approach to quantify the gating effect.
proposals.contribution: This project will establish design principles for switchable diffusion in non-crystalline porous systems, expanding the toolkit for responsive materials beyond MOFs. It will provide fundamental insights into how dynamic disorder can be harnessed for gas separation and sorption, potentially leading to more durable and scalable materials for vapor capture.
proposals.novelty: The approach deliberately moves away from crystalline frameworks to exploit dynamic disorder for gating, a paradigm shift from Kitagawa's work on crystalline soft porous crystals. Combining amorphous polymers with the nascent concept of porous liquids for water vapor regulation is a novel and unexplored strategy.

proposals.title: A Soft Porous Coordination Polymer with Temperature-Inverted Gate-Opening Adsorption for C2H2/CO2 Separation
proposals.motivation: The separation of acetylene (C2H2) from carbon dioxide (CO2) is critical for industrial production of polymers and chemicals, but remains challenging due to their similar molecular dimensions and kinetic diameters. Conventional adsorbents typically show a trade-off between selectivity and capacity, with selectivity often decreasing at higher temperatures. An adsorbent that inverts this paradigm, selectively capturing C2H2 at low temperature and CO2 at high temperature, could enable a novel, energy-efficient temperature-swing separation process.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a flexible, nitro-functionalized soft porous crystal (SPC) can exhibit a temperature-inverted, two-step gate-opening adsorption, where the framework's structural transition for C2H2 is entropically favored at low temperatures, while CO2 adsorption is enthalpically dominant and activated at higher temperatures.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a series of 2D layered coordination polymers using a nitro-functionalized, bent dicarboxylate linker (inspired by Kitagawa's recent work on nitro-programmed SPCs) and pillared with flexible bipyridine ligands. High-pressure sorption isotherms for C2H2 and CO2 will be measured from 273 K to 323 K to map the temperature-dependent selectivity inversion. In-situ powder X-ray diffraction and calorimetry will correlate structural dynamics and binding energies. Grand Canonical Monte Carlo simulations, using pre-2026 force fields, will model the density of states and entropy contributions to binding.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver the first prototype of a porous material exhibiting a fully inverted temperature-selectivity relationship for C2H2/CO2. It will provide fundamental insight into how entropic effects from framework flexibility can dominate selectivity at low temperatures, advancing the design principles for dynamic adsorbents. The proposed material could form the basis for a one-step purification process with reduced energy penalty.
proposals.novelty: While temperature-dependent selectivity is known, intentionally designing a material where the preferred guest inverts with temperature is a non-obvious and unexplored strategy for gas separation, moving beyond static pore chemistry to harness dynamic, entropy-driven recognition.

proposals.title: Light- and Electric-Field-Responsive Soft Porous Crystals for On-Demand Hydrocarbon Separation
proposals.motivation: Stimuli-responsive porous materials, particularly soft porous crystals (SPCs), offer transformative potential for energy-efficient separation processes. While Kitagawa's group has pioneered SPCs responsive to guest molecules (e.g., hydrocarbons) and temperature, integrating non-thermal external triggers like light or electric fields remains a significant challenge. Developing such materials would enable precise, remote, and instantaneous control over adsorption and separation, moving beyond passive, equilibrium-based processes.
proposals.hypothesis: By strategically integrating photochromic azobenzene moieties or redox-active viologen units into the organic linkers of metal-organic frameworks (MOFs), we can create SPCs whose pore geometry and adsorption affinity for C2-C3 hydrocarbons can be reversibly switched by light irradiation or applied electric potential.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a series of pillared-layer MOFs using Zn2+ or Cu2+ paddle-wheel clusters and ditopic linkers functionalized with azobenzene or diquat (viologen precursor). For light-responsive systems, we will use UV/Vis light to trigger cis-trans isomerization. For electric-field response, we will fabricate thin films of the viologen-based MOF on conductive substrates to apply a potential, inducing a redox-state change. Sorption isotherms for C2H4, C2H6, C3H6, and C3H8 will be measured under alternating stimuli, and structural dynamics will be monitored via in-situ powder X-ray diffraction and infrared spectroscopy.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish the first design principles for MOFs with direct, reversible pore modulation via light and electric fields, expanding the toolkit for dynamic separation. It will provide fundamental insights into the coupling between external stimuli, framework flexibility, and guest selectivity, directly contributing to the development of next-generation, on-demand separation systems.
proposals.novelty: While stimuli-response in MOFs is known, creating a single material platform where pore aperture and binding energy are directly and reversibly tunable by two distinct, non-thermal external triggers (light and electric field) for hydrocarbon separation is unprecedented. This moves beyond the established paradigm of guest- or temperature-induced flexibility.

proposals.title: Enhancing Cyclic Stability of Soft Porous Crystals for Temperature-Swing Hydrocarbon Separation via Ligand and Node Reinforcement
proposals.motivation: Soft porous crystals (SPCs) like the nitro-functionalized frameworks pioneered by Kitagawa's group show exceptional selectivity for olefin/paraffin separations via gate-opening adsorption. However, their practical application in industrial temperature-swing adsorption (TSA) cycles is hindered by potential framework degradation from repeated thermal and adsorption stress, especially in the presence of water vapor. Achieving long-term cyclic stability is a critical, unresolved challenge for deploying these intelligent materials in energy-efficient separation processes.
proposals.hypothesis: Strategic reinforcement of the metal-organic framework (MOF) through the incorporation of rigid, hydrophobic co-ligands and the formation of robust, high-connectivity metal clusters will significantly enhance the chemical and mechanical resilience of the framework without compromising its dynamic, stimuli-responsive separation performance.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a series of isoreticular SPCs based on the prototypal nitro-functionalized ligand, systematically introducing rigid aromatic co-ligands (e.g., bipyridine) and employing metal clusters (e.g., paddlewheel Cu2 or trinuclear clusters) known for high thermal stability. High-temperature water vapor sorption and dynamic hydrocarbon (C3H6/C3H8) adsorption-desorption cycles (25-120°C) will be performed over hundreds of cycles. Framework integrity will be monitored in situ via PXRD and gas sorption analysis, complemented by density functional theory (DFT) calculations to model stress points and binding energy landscapes during cyclic operation.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish clear design principles for stabilizing dynamic frameworks against cyclic operational stress, directly addressing a major bottleneck in their industrial translation. It will yield one or more prototype materials with validated long-term TSA performance, advancing the field from demonstrating selective adsorption to proving viable cyclic process operation.
proposals.novelty: While prior work focuses on inducing flexibility or tuning gate-opening pressure, this proposal uniquely targets the systematic engineering of framework *stability* for the dynamic operating conditions of TSA, merging concepts of mechanical reinforcement from rigid MOFs with the programmable flexibility of SPCs.

proposals.title: Thermally Programmable Gate Adsorption in Soft Porous Crystals via LCST/UCST-Type Dynamic Organic Moieties
proposals.motivation: The development of porous materials with dynamically responsive pore apertures is crucial for advanced gas separation and sensing, particularly for energy-efficient purification of hydrocarbons like propylene. While Kitagawa's work on soft porous crystals (SPCs) has demonstrated structural flexibility, the trigger mechanisms are often limited to guest-induced transformations. Integrating the well-established stimuli-responsiveness of polymers, specifically lower/upper critical solution temperature (LCST/UCST) behavior, into the organic struts of metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) or hybrid ultramicroporous materials presents a novel route to engineer intrinsic, temperature-programmable adsorption gates.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that incorporating organic linkers functionalized with oligomeric or dendritic side chains exhibiting LCST- or UCST-type phase behavior in a solvent-like environment (e.g., within pores) will impart a sharp, thermally controlled hydrophilic/hydrophobic switching to the pore walls. This will directly modulate the host-guest interactions and the effective pore aperture for small molecules like water and hydrocarbons, creating a new class of thermally responsive adsorbents.
proposals.method: We will design and synthesize a series of bis-pyrazole or bis-triazole organic linkers, functionalized with oligo(ethylene glycol) (OEG, for LCST-type) or sulfobetaine (for UCST-type) moieties. These will be reacted with metal salts (e.g., Cu(I), Zn(II)) under solvothermal conditions to form new SPCs. The materials will be characterized by SCXRD, PXRD, and TGA. Water and hydrocarbon (C2/C3) vapor sorption isotherms will be measured dynamically across a temperature range (e.g., 5-80°C) to map the thermal switching of adsorption capacity and gate-opening pressure. In-situ PXRD and solid-state NMR will correlate structural changes with sorption behavior.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a foundational design principle for creating intrinsically thermoresponsive porous materials by merging polymer science concepts with coordination chemistry. It will deliver new porous platforms with predictable, sharp adsorption on/off switches controlled by a simple thermal stimulus, advancing the field of dynamic porous materials beyond guest-induced responses. The resulting materials have direct potential for thermally cycled adsorption processes and smart sensor interfaces.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the deliberate molecular-level integration of LCST/UCST polymer physics into the dynamic organic component of a porous coordination framework, a concept not yet explored in Kitagawa's or others' work on SPCs. This moves beyond post-synthetic modification or simple thermal expansion to encode a cooperative, phase-transition-like response directly into the pore wall chemistry.

proposals.title: Single-Molecule Gas Discrimination via Dynamic Electrical Fingerprinting in a Tailored Soft Porous Coordination Polymer
proposals.motivation: Chemiresistive sensors based on porous materials like MOFs offer promise for trace gas detection, but ensemble averaging over many pores obscures the unique binding dynamics of individual analyte molecules. Achieving single-molecule resolution would enable unprecedented selectivity and sensitivity for early disease detection via breath analysis or environmental monitoring, moving beyond bulk sorption isotherms to fundamental host-guest physics.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a single, electrically addressable pore within a soft porous coordination polymer (PCP) will exhibit distinct, molecule-specific transient electrical signatures (dynamic 'fingerprints') as individual gas molecules adsorb, interact, and desorb, allowing for definitive molecular discrimination.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a 2D electroactive PCP with diamondoid topology, known for flexible, ultramicroporous frameworks responsive to guests like water and hydrocarbons. A single crystalline flake will be integrated into a nanofabricated electrode gap. Using a custom high-temporal-resolution (µs) source-measure unit, we will monitor real-time conductance fluctuations at elevated temperature (50-100°C) to enhance dynamics. Signal processing will employ wavelet transform analysis and hidden Markov modeling to deconvolute and classify discrete binding events from noise, correlating specific signal patterns (duration, amplitude, shape) to individual molecules (e.g., H2O, C3H6, NO2).
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new paradigm for gas sensing by directly linking single-molecule binding kinetics to a measurable electronic output in a porous material. It will provide foundational data on the stochastic dynamics of guest inclusion in flexible frameworks and demonstrate a path toward ultimate-limit sensors with digital, molecule-by-molecule resolution.
proposals.novelty: While Kitagawa's group has studied bulk sorption properties and ensemble sensor responses, applying single-molecule electrical measurement techniques to a *dynamic*, soft porous crystal—where pore geometry adapts to the guest—for real-time molecular fingerprinting is a novel and unexplored frontier.

ideas.title: Employ stimuli-responsive polymer concepts for crystal design
ideas.description: Translate principles from temperature-sensitive polymers to design organic linkers for porous crystals.
ideas.sourceTitle: Delicate Softness in a Temperature-Responsive Porous Crystal for Accelerated Sieving of Propylene/Propane.

ideas.title: Hierarchically Structured COF Nanosheet Composites with Tunable Interlayer and Pore Environments
ideas.description: This research direction focuses on the synthesis of monolayer or few-layer Covalent Organic Framework (COF) nanosheets and their assembly into hierarchical composite catalysts. The key innovation is to use the COF nanosheets as modular, two-dimensional components that can be stacked, intercalated, or integrated with other nanomaterials (e.g., conductive polymers, metal nanoparticles, or graphene) in a precisely controlled manner. This allows for the systematic tuning of composite properties—such as charge transport pathways, active site accessibility, and chemical stability—by manipulating the interactions between the nanosheet layers and the composite matrix. The resulting materials are designed to offer unprecedented control over fuel cell catalysis, where the microenvironment around each active site can be optimized dynamically for specific reactions like the oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) or alcohol oxidation.

ideas.title: Feedback-optimized dynamic combinatorial chemistry for kinetically trapped COFs
ideas.description: This redesigned system integrates dynamic combinatorial library (DCL) screening with in-situ, real-time monitoring of both molecular library composition and the evolving material properties of the forming covalent organic frameworks (COFs). A closed-loop feedback system uses analytical data (e.g., HPLC for library speciation, PXRD for crystallinity) to algorithmically adjust reaction kinetics and thermodynamics, such as temperature or catalyst concentration. This actively steers the DCL equilibrium away from merely the most thermodynamically stable complexes and toward the building blocks that produce COFs with optimal target characteristics like high crystallinity, porosity, and stability.

ideas.title: Closed-loop autonomous platform for COF discovery integrating robotic synthesis with real-time characterization and adaptive AI guidance
ideas.description: This system couples high-throughput robotic synthesis for covalent organic frameworks with integrated, automated material characterization tools within the same workflow. An AI controller analyzes real-time data from in-line powder X-ray diffraction and gas sorption analysis to assess crystallinity and porosity immediately after synthesis. Using this feedback, the AI adaptively designs the next set of experiments, prioritizing reaction spaces that yield well-defined, porous materials and avoiding barren regions. This closed-loop approach ensures that high-throughput experimentation generates immediately validated, high-quality materials data, dramatically accelerating the reliable discovery of novel COFs with targeted properties.

ideas.title: Inverse Design of Kinetically Trapped, MOF-Inspired Architectures for Robust Composite Catalysts
ideas.description: This project computationally reverse-engineers the optimal pore architecture and site distribution of a hypothetical MOF catalyst for a target reaction. Instead of synthesizing the MOF, this digital blueprint directs the assembly of durable composite precursors. Using sequential, irreversible reactions—such as controlled cross-linking of polymers within a sacrificial template or atomic layer deposition on designed scaffolds—the system kinetically traps the MOF-inspired nanoarchitecture into a covalently bonded, amorphous or microcrystalline composite. This yields a catalyst with the precise spatial design benefits of a MOF but with the thermal stability and chemical resilience of engineered ceramics or carbon matrices, enabling application in demanding catalytic processes like high-temperature reforming or oxidative desulfurization.

ideas.title: Template-Guided Irreversible Synthesis of Multi-Component COFs via Orthogonal Reversible Interactions
ideas.description: This approach decouples the need for reversibility in the final COF bonds from the need for reversibility in the assembly process. A multi-component system is designed where the target, permanent COF linkages form irreversibly, but their spatial arrangement is directed by a separate set of reversible, template-based interactions. These guiding interactions (e.g., metal-ligand coordination on a supramolecular scaffold or hydrogen-bonded assembly of precursors) provide the necessary error correction and long-range order during crystallization. After the irreversible COF framework forms around this template, the template is selectively removed, yielding a highly crystalline, multi-component COF whose stability is not dependent on the reversibility of its primary covalent bonds.

ideas.title: Vitrimer-Grafted Covalent Organic Frameworks for Processable Crystalline Composites
ideas.description: This redesigned approach fabricates processable COF-based materials by chemically grafting a thin layer of a vitrimer network directly onto the external surfaces of pre-synthesized COF crystallites. The grafted vitrimer layer, rich in dynamic covalent bonds (e.g., transesterification), acts as an adaptive interfacial glue between rigid COF particles. Upon heating, bond exchange within this compliant interfacial phase allows the COF crystallites to rearrange and flow relative to one another, enabling shaping, welding, and recycling of the macroscopic material. This preserves the high crystallinity, permanent porosity, and chemical functionality of the COF domains while overcoming the intrinsic processability limitations of purely crystalline frameworks.

ideas.title: Assume organic ligands limit stability

ideas.title: Synthesize polymer-crosslinked, mechanically reinforced monolayer COF nanosheets on 2D templates
ideas.description: This approach overcomes the fragility and restacking of conventional COF nanosheets by first growing a monolayer COF via surface-confined synthesis on a 2D crystalline template. A key innovation is the subsequent in-situ infiltration and cross-linking of a secondary, flexible polymer network that forms covalent bonds with the COF's structural nodes, acting as a molecular 'reinforcing mesh'. This creates a robust, free-standing hybrid nanosheet after template etching, which maintains high surface area, prevents restacking due to the polymer spacers, and exhibits superior mechanical integrity for direct device fabrication and durable catalytic use.

ideas.title: Optimize framework stability for cyclic operation

ideas.title: Applying Metallurgical Phase Diagram Control to Covalent Organic Framework Vitrimerization
ideas.description: This idea adapts the principles of metallurgical phase diagram control to solve the problem of managing reversibility and processing in multi-component Covalent Organic Framework (COF) vitrimers. In metallurgy, the properties of complex alloys are engineered by precisely controlling composition and thermal history according to established phase diagrams, enabling predictable solid-state transformations and reprocessing. By applying this analogical framework, we propose to systematically map the dynamic covalent chemistry of multi-component COFs (e.g., mixtures of boronic esters, imines) onto a similar 'composition-processing-property' phase diagram. This adapted approach would allow for the predictive design of COF vitrimers with precisely tuned topological freezing temperatures and viscoelastic properties, facilitating controlled reprocessing, annealing of crystalline domains, and defect healing—effectively treating the COF network like a molecular-scale 'covalent alloy' whose dynamic behavior can be engineered for specific applications.

ideas.title: Irreversible COF Architectures for Inverse-Designed Fuel Cell Electrodes
ideas.description: This research direction challenges the dogma of full reversibility in covalent organic framework (COF) synthesis by strategically employing irreversible steps to create multi-component COF composites with novel, metastable phases and domain structures. These synthesized composites possess finely tuned but non-equilibrium properties (e.g., proton conductivity, catalyst dispersion, stability) that become the design constraints. An inverse problem approach is then applied, treating these composite properties as fixed targets to computationally design and fabricate custom fuel cell electrode architectures—including microstructural gradients and interfacial layers—that are uniquely optimized to exploit the composite's specific advantages, thereby breaking the conventional cycle of adapting catalysts to standard cell designs.

ideas.title: Assume MOFs are not the primary functional component

ideas.title: Enhance catalytic performance via bio-inspired hierarchical porosity and compartmentalization
ideas.description: Drawing an analogy from biocatalysis, where enzyme cascade efficiency is boosted by spatial organization within hierarchical scaffolds, this idea proposes to enhance the catalytic performance of composite materials for fuel cell reactions. The analogous solution involves co-localizing enzymes in compartmentalized, porous structures to enable substrate channeling and microenvironment control. Adapted to this context, the research idea is to design and synthesize composite catalysts with bio-inspired, hierarchical porosity that physically compartmentalizes distinct catalytic sites (e.g., for oxygen reduction and methanol oxidation reactions). This engineered architecture aims to minimize diffusion limitations, protect active components, and create ideal local chemical environments, thereby synergistically improving catalytic activity, selectivity, and durability for fuel cell applications.

ideas.title: Bio-inspired hierarchical composite catalysts using earth-abundant core-shell structures
ideas.description: This research seed expands the original idea to reduce the cost of composite catalysts by drawing an analogy from biomineralization in structural biomaterials. The analogous field shows how nature combines tiny amounts of organic polymer with abundant minerals to create high-performance composites like nacre. The analogous solution is the use of organic templates to control the hierarchical assembly of cheap inorganic components. Adapted to catalysis, the idea is to develop lower-cost composite catalysts by using sacrificial or conductive organic polymers to template the formation of core-shell nanostructures from earth-abundant catalytic metals and metal oxides. This bio-inspired approach aims to create hierarchically porous, stable, and active composites where the polymer matrix enhances electron transfer and structural integrity, effectively replacing expensive platinum-based materials through smart material architecture rather than relying on costly noble metals.

ideas.title: High-Throughput Screening of Amorphous-Crystalline Composite Photocatalysts
ideas.description: This research direction applies high-throughput combinatorial screening methods from drug discovery to rapidly synthesize and test libraries of composite photocatalysts that intentionally combine amorphous and crystalline phases with the same chemical linkage. The core objective is to systematically map how amorphous polymer domains, when formulated as precise additives or coatings within a crystalline photocatalytic framework, influence overall photoactivity—testing the hypothesis that amorphicity can be beneficial in a composite context. This approach allows for the efficient optimization of parameters like amorphous-to-crystalline ratio, domain size, and interfacial engineering to maximize synergistic effects such as improved charge carrier separation or tailored surface adsorption sites. The result is a data-driven platform for designing non-traditional photocatalysts where the amorphous component is not a flaw but a functionally optimized element of a composite system.

ideas.title: Apply high-throughput computational screening from catalysis
ideas.description: Use DFT and machine learning to predict optimal bimetallic MOF compositions for EM absorption.
ideas.sourceTitle: Atomic Tuning in Electrically Conducting Bimetallic Organic Frameworks for Controllable Electromagnetic Wave Absorption

ideas.title: Systematic Decoupling of Crystallinity and Linkage Chemistry in Conjugated Polymers for Photocatalysis
ideas.description: This research direction systematically synthesizes a series of conjugated polymers where the buta-1,3-diene linkage and its structural analogs (e.g., vinylene, phenylene-vinylene, acetylene) are incorporated into both highly crystalline and deliberately amorphous polymer backbones. By comparing photocatalytic performance (e.g., hydrogen evolution, pollutant degradation) across this two-dimensional design space, the work will uniquely determine if the purported superiority of the butadiene linkage is an intrinsic electronic effect or if it is contingent upon achieving a specific crystalline packing. This approach moves beyond binary comparisons to establish fundamental structure-property relationships that can guide the rational design of disordered, solution-processable photocatalysts without sacrificing activity.

ideas.title: Invert the core-sheath material roles

ideas.title: Amorphous Polymer Matrices as Platinum-Free, High-Performance Photocatalytic Platforms
ideas.description: This research fuses the challenge to platinum's necessity with the exploration of amorphous photocatalytic materials. The core hypothesis is that the flexible, defect-rich local environments in amorphous conjugated polymers can be engineered to create active sites that mimic or surpass the catalytic functions of platinum in key reactions like hydrogen evolution or oxygen reduction. By systematically synthesizing amorphous polymers with the same linkages as their crystalline counterparts but varying local structure, we can decouple the effects of long-range order from metal-free catalytic activity. This approach aims to discover a new class of entirely platinum-free, high-performance photocatalysts where disorder is not a detriment but a design parameter for creating novel catalytic centers.

ideas.title: Adapting Multi-Component Polymerization Strategies to Diversify COF Linkages
ideas.description: This idea proposes to extend the multi-component synthesis strategy for COFs by drawing an analogy from the use of multi-component reactions (MCRs) in organic synthesis and medicinal chemistry. In that field, MCRs efficiently generate molecular diversity by combining multiple reactants in one pot to form new, complex scaffolds. Adapting this solution, we would design COF syntheses that employ three or more distinct molecular building blocks which participate in two or more different, yet compatible, dynamic covalent reactions simultaneously. This approach moves beyond creating COFs with a single, non-traditional linkage and instead aims to fabricate frameworks with heterogeneous, interwoven linkage types (e.g., a COF with both imine and boroxine linkages) in a single synthetic step, thereby accessing unprecedented structural complexity and tailored material properties.

ideas.title: Single-Atom Composite Catalysts via DNA Origami Scaffolding
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution from structural biology and nanotechnology—specifically, the programmable self-assembly of DNA origami—to the challenge of constructing single-atom composite catalysts. In the analogous field, DNA origami scaffolds solve the problem of positioning different functional components with atomic-scale precision to create complex machines. The adapted research idea is to use synthetic DNA nanostructures as templates or direct scaffolds to organize two or more different types of single metal atoms (e.g., Pt and Co, or Fe and Cu) into a specific, stable composite active site on a conductive or catalytic support. This method would allow for the rational design of the catalyst's geometric and electronic structure at the single-atom level, enabling the systematic study and optimization of synergistic effects for maximum catalytic efficiency in reactions like oxygen reduction or selective oxidation.

ideas.title: Invert the temperature-selectivity relationship

ideas.title: Scalable Manufacturing of Composite Catalysts for Diversified Multi-Component Reaction Platforms
ideas.description: This research direction focuses on designing and manufacturing robust, modular composite catalysts (e.g., supported organocatalysts, bifunctional acid-base nanomaterials) using scalable techniques like continuous flow impregnation or spray drying. These catalysts are specifically engineered to be compatible with a broad palette of nucleophiles and electrophiles beyond the classic aldehyde/acetonitrile pair, such as ketones, activated olefins, and heterocyclic amines. The synergy lies in using the scalable manufacturing process to produce catalyst libraries with tunable properties, which are then deployed to efficiently probe and optimize new MCR pathways under industrially relevant conditions from the outset. This integrated approach simultaneously solves the catalyst supply bottleneck and accelerates the development of new, practical multi-component syntheses for complex molecules.

ideas.title: Challenging the Platinum Scaffold: A Bio-inspired Approach to Catalyst Design
ideas.description: This idea challenges the necessity of platinum in composite catalysts by drawing an analogy from structural biology, where expensive metal cofactors in enzymes are replaced by designing protein scaffolds that reconfigure abundant metals. The analogous solution involves computational design of active sites and scaffold engineering. Adapted to catalysis, the research would computationally define the essential catalytic 'pocket' for a reaction like fuel cell oxygen reduction, then design and synthesize a composite material where a cheap, abundant metal center is forced into a specific geometry and electronic state by a rigid, chemically tunable support matrix. The goal is to achieve acceptable or superior performance by replicating the function, not the specific element, of platinum, thereby breaking the direct link between performance and platinum content.

ideas.title: ML-Guided Dynamic Combinatorial Chemistry for Self-Optimizing Covalent Organic Framework Catalysts
ideas.description: This research direction integrates machine learning (ML) for catalyst prediction with the principles of dynamic combinatorial chemistry (DCC) applied to covalent organic frameworks (COFs). An ML model, pre-trained on existing material databases, is used to design an initial library of building blocks and predict the catalytic performance of their possible COF assemblies. This library is then subjected to DCC screening, where reversible reactions under a catalytic target reaction (e.g., CO2 reduction) drive the dynamic system to enrich the most effective COF structures. The real-time, compositional data from the DCC experiment is fed back to iteratively retrain and refine the ML model, creating a synergistic feedback loop that accelerates the discovery of high-performance, multi-component COF catalysts beyond the scope of static prediction or conventional screening alone.

ideas.title: Adapt High-Throughput Robotic Synthesis Screening for COFs Using a Microfluidic Droplet Platform
ideas.description: This idea expands the original concept of transferring pharmaceutical high-throughput screening to COF synthesis by drawing a direct analogy from the field of directed evolution of enzymes. In that field, the analogous problem of screening vast libraries of protein variants for function is solved using microfluidic droplet platforms coupled with fluorescence-activated sorting. We adapt this solution to the research context of covalent organic frameworks (COFs). An automated robotic platform will be used to generate millions of discrete microdroplets, each acting as a microreactor for a distinct multi-component COF synthesis condition (varying linkers, catalysts, solvents, concentrations). An integrated real-time analytical readout (e.g., static light scattering for particle formation or fluorescence from an incorporated probe) will allow a sorting module to physically isolate only the droplets where successful polymerization and crystallization occur, dramatically accelerating the discovery and optimization of new COF materials.

ideas.title: Engineering Local Order in Amorphous Photocatalysts via Defect Templating
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that long-range crystallinity is mandatory for high photoactivity in polymeric photocatalysts. Drawing an analogy from metallic glass engineering, where controlled atomic-scale heterogeneities are introduced to enhance material properties without crystallization, we propose a parallel strategy for amorphous polymers. The analogous solution involves creating specific, localized order via defect engineering. Adapted to photocatalysis, the research would explore synthesizing amorphous polymers with the same linkage but incorporating designed molecular templates or structural defects during polymerization. These engineered sites aim to create localized domains with optimal electronic structure for charge carrier generation and separation, potentially restoring photocatalytic efficiency rivaling crystalline materials, while retaining the beneficial processability and isotropic properties of the amorphous state.

ideas.title: Apply Fragment-Based Drug Discovery (FBDD) Screening Logic to COF Building Block Selection
ideas.description: This idea adapts the core methodology of Fragment-Based Drug Discovery (FBDD) from pharmaceutical science to the discovery of optimal building blocks for Covalent Organic Frameworks (COFs). In the analogous field, weak-binding molecular fragments are identified against a protein target and then optimized. The adapted solution involves using a representative COF structure or a key synthetic intermediate as a static 'target' during the dynamic combinatorial chemistry process. A library of simple, reversible monomers (e.g., aldehydes, amines) is allowed to equilibrate in the presence of this target. Analysis of the shifted equilibrium, using techniques like HPLC or mass spectrometry, identifies which primitive 'fragment' building blocks are preferentially selected or templated by the target structure. These identified optimal fragments then serve as the foundational units for the rational design of more complex, multi-topic linkers, streamlining the discovery of COFs with enhanced crystallinity, stability, or functional properties.

ideas.title: Enzymatic-Inspired Catalysis for Ambient COF Synthesis
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that multi-component COFs require solvothermal conditions by drawing an analogy from enzyme catalysis in biochemistry. Enzymes synthesize complex molecules in water at ambient temperature by creating a tailored active site. The adapted research idea is to design COF precursors with built-in, transient catalytic functionalities that mimic an enzyme's active site, organizing components and catalyzing the linkage formation at room temperature or via mild mechanochemical activation, thereby enabling alternative, energy-efficient synthetic pathways.

ideas.title: Diagnostics-Enabled Feedback Control for Crystalline COF Synthesis Under Mild Conditions
ideas.description: This project develops a closed-loop synthesis platform that combines alternative, mild-condition methods (e.g., mechanochemical, vapor-assisted) with integrated real-time analytical diagnostics. The system uses in-situ X-ray diffraction and spectroscopic probes to continuously monitor the evolution of crystallinity and chemical bonding during the reaction. A feedback control algorithm then dynamically adjusts mechanical energy input, vapor pressure, or stoichiometry to steer the synthesis toward highly crystalline, low-defect COFs. This approach guarantees that materials produced under mild conditions meet or exceed the structural quality of solvothermally synthesized COFs, validating the viability of the alternative routes.

ideas.title: Scalable, Low-Cost Composite COF Photocatalysts for Continuous Flow Reactors
ideas.description: This research fuses the need for bulk COF synthesis for flow reactors with the imperative of cost-effective composite design. The core objective is to develop scalable, continuous manufacturing processes for buta-1,3-diene COF-based composites, where the COF is integrated with low-cost, earth-abundant co-catalysts and supports (e.g., carbon materials, metal oxides) to form a robust, high-surface-area photocatalytic material. The work will systematically evaluate the trade-offs between composite synthesis complexity, photocatalytic efficiency for target reactions (e.g., hydrogen evolution, organic transformations), and long-term stability under continuous flow conditions. The ultimate goal is to establish a materials platform that simultaneously achieves the processability required for reactor engineering and a compelling cost-to-performance ratio that surpasses platinum-based systems.

ideas.title: Enhancing Composite Catalyst Stability via Bio-Inspired Encapsulation and Self-Healing Matrices
ideas.description: This research idea applies an analogy from biomedical drug delivery to address composite catalyst stability in fuel cells. In drug delivery, fragile active agents are protected within engineered polymer matrices to enhance their longevity and function in harsh biological environments. The adapted solution involves designing and synthesizing a protective, nano-porous, and electrically conductive encapsulation matrix (e.g., from tailored conductive polymers or stable MOFs) around the core catalyst particles within the composite. This matrix would serve as a durable physical barrier against catalyst degradation mechanisms like Ostwald ripening, particle coalescence, and corrosive attack, while its engineered porosity and conductivity would maintain necessary mass and charge transport. The investigation would focus on matrix material selection, encapsulation techniques, and evaluating the long-term electrochemical stability and performance of these shielded composite catalysts under realistic fuel cell operating conditions.

ideas.title: Design of Interface-Stabilized Composite Catalysts for Durable Fuel Cell Reactions
ideas.description: This research aims to overcome the critical durability bottleneck in composite catalysts by fundamentally engineering their internal interfaces for long-term stability. It integrates operando spectroscopy and microscopy to diagnose failure mechanisms at the nanoscale, guiding the synthesis of composites with covalent or epitaxial linkages that prevent component segregation. High-throughput computational screening is used to identify thermodynamically favorable interface structures under operational electrochemical potentials. The final output is a materials design rule set for creating composite catalysts that maintain high activity and efficiency over extended operational lifetimes, moving beyond initial performance metrics.

ideas.title: Designing Stabilized Single-Atom Composite Catalysts via Atomic-Scale Support Engineering
ideas.description: This research investigates the fundamental mechanisms of degradation in single-atom catalysts (SACs) for fuel cells and designs composite supports to mitigate them. Instead of viewing the support as a passive anchor, we engineer it as an active, multi-component matrix that uses synergistic interactions (e.g., strong metal-support bonding, charge transfer, and protective secondary phases) to immobilize single atoms and shield them from agglomeration, leaching, and poisoning. The core synergy is that the composite nature of the support is explicitly optimized for atomic-scale stability, transforming the pursuit of maximum efficiency into one of inherently durable efficiency. This approach aims to establish design principles for SACs where long-term operational stability is built into the material's architecture from the single atom upwards.

ideas.title: Multi-Objective Mapping of Conjugated Linkages for Targeted Photocatalytic Function
ideas.description: This project moves beyond seeking a universally optimal linkage to defining which conjugated bridge is best for a specific photocatalytic task. A diverse set of organic photocatalysts, differing only in their core conjugated linker, is synthesized and characterized through automated optical and electrochemical assays. The resulting dataset is analyzed to correlate linker structure with key performance metrics, revealing trade-offs and enabling rational design. The outcome is a predictive framework for selecting the ideal linkage based on application-specific requirements like desired excitation wavelength or necessary charge separation efficiency.

ideas.title: High-Throughput Discovery of Single-Atom-Alloy Composite Catalysts
ideas.description: This research direction integrates the precision of single-atom composite catalysts with the speed of high-throughput combinatorial screening borrowed from pharmaceutical discovery. It establishes an automated synthesis and testing pipeline to generate libraries of composite materials where single atoms of different transition metals are precisely anchored on varied, engineered support matrices. By applying rapid electrochemical or spectroscopic screening, the platform can correlate atomic-scale compositional and structural descriptors with catalytic performance across thousands of unique formulations. This data-driven approach aims to uncover non-intuitive, highly active, and stable single-atom-alloy composites for complex reactions like CO2 reduction or methane activation, moving beyond trial-and-error to accelerated rational design.

ideas.title: Expand Multi-Component Reaction Scope via a Modular Ligand Screening Platform
ideas.description: The original idea challenges the assumption that the multi-component scheme is limited to aldehydes and acetonitrile by proposing to replace them with other nucleophiles or electrophiles. Drawing an analogy from homogeneous catalysis, where modular ligand libraries and high-throughput screening solved the problem of narrow substrate scope in cross-coupling reactions, we adapt this solution. The adapted research idea is to expand the multi-component reaction scope via a modular component screening platform. This involves creating libraries of diverse potential nucleophiles and electrophiles and using high-throughput parallel synthesis to systematically test their compatibility in the reaction scheme. This methodical, discovery-oriented approach will efficiently identify viable new component pairs, fundamentally broadening the applicability of the multi-component synthesis beyond the original aldehyde/acetonitrile constraint.

ideas.title: Inverse Design of Continuous Flow Photocatalytic Fuel Cells
ideas.description: This research direction integrates the inverse design philosophy of Idea A with the scale-up and continuous flow objectives of Idea B. The core concept is to design and fabricate a continuous flow photocatalytic reactor that also functions as a fuel cell, specifically architected around the unique charge generation, separation, and transport properties of the bulk-produced buta-1,3-diene COF. This co-design approach optimizes the reactor's internal geometry, flow paths, and electrode interfaces not just for high-yield chemical synthesis, but also for the efficient harvesting of photogenerated electrons to produce electrical power. The result is a dual-purpose, scalable system that simultaneously synthesizes a valuable chemical and converts solar energy directly into both chemical and electrical energy.
